proposals.title: Mapping the Micro-Contexts of Engagement: An Ecological Momentary Assessment of Spatially Talented Students in STEM Activities
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on spatial talent, a critical predictor of STEM success, relies heavily on static, lab-based assessments and retrospective self-reports, which fail to capture the dynamic, context-dependent nature of academic engagement and behavioral challenges in real-world learning environments. This gap limits our understanding of how and why engagement fluctuates for these students, hindering the development of timely, personalized educational supports. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), proven in clinical and behavioral sciences pre-2026, offers a powerful but underutilized method to study these real-time processes in educational settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially talented students will exhibit unique, context-sensitive patterns of engagement and challenge, characterized by high engagement during hands-on, spatially demanding STEM activities but significant behavioral challenges (e.g., frustration, off-task behavior) during routine or verbally-focused academic tasks, as captured via smartphone-based EMA.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 spatially talented middle/high school students, identified via a pre-validated spatial ability battery (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT). Using a custom smartphone app, participants will receive 4-5 random prompts daily over 14 school days, assessing current activity (e.g., math lecture, engineering project), engagement (visual analog scales), and challenge (Likert-scale items on focus, frustration). GPS and accelerometer data will provide objective context. Analysis will employ multilevel modeling (HLM) to model within-person fluctuations, with spatial ability scores and activity type (coded via a pre-2026 STEM task taxonomy) as key predictors of momentary engagement and challenge states.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, within-person account of the daily academic experiences of spatially talented students, moving beyond trait-level descriptions to identify situational triggers for engagement and disengagement. It will contribute a validated, technology-enabled EMA protocol for educational research and generate actionable insights for designing just-in-time interventions and tailoring STEM outreach activities to sustain the talent development of this population.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in its application of clinical psychology's EMA methodology to the study of spatial talent—a construct typically assessed statically—and its integration of objective sensor data with subjective prompts to create a rich, contextualized picture of student experience that existing survey-based or observational methods cannot achieve.

proposals.title: Micro-Genetic Case Studies of Spatially Talented Students: Unpacking Daily Classroom Experiences and Instructional Interactions
proposals.motivation: Research on spatial ability in STEM education has largely relied on large-scale assessments and cross-sectional designs, which identify correlations but fail to capture the nuanced, day-to-day classroom experiences of spatially talented students. Understanding these daily interactions is critical for developing effective, in-the-moment pedagogical strategies that can nurture this talent, especially given pre-2026 calls for personalized learning and talent development in K-12 STEM.
proposals.hypothesis: Spatially talented students experience and navigate typical STEM classroom activities through distinct, observable patterns of reasoning and engagement, and their talent development is significantly influenced by specific, often fleeting, teacher-student instructional interactions.
proposals.method: This study will employ a micro-genetic, qualitative case study design in two middle school STEM classrooms. Data collection will involve intensive video ethnography of daily lessons over one instructional unit, supplemented by pre/post spatial ability assessments (using established instruments like the PSVT:R), targeted think-aloud protocols during spatial tasks, student and teacher interviews, and artifact analysis. Video data will be analyzed using a structured, iterative coding process, combining deductive codes from spatial cognition frameworks (e.g., mental rotation, spatial visualization) and inductive thematic analysis to identify patterns in student reasoning, discourse, and teacher responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rich, contextualized model of how spatial talent manifests and is shaped in authentic classroom settings, moving beyond test scores to observable behaviors and interactions. It will generate a framework of 'instructional sensitivity points'—specific moments in lessons where targeted teacher actions can significantly impact spatially talented students—directly informing professional development for educators and the design of more responsive classroom activities.
proposals.novelty: While spatial assessment scales are common, applying a micro-genetic, ethnographic lens to study the daily lived experience of spatially talented students in intact classrooms is novel. This approach bridges the gap between large-scale psychometric research and the granular realities of teaching practice, focusing on process over product.

proposals.title: Cross-Cultural Validation of the Belief, Skill, Knowledge (BSK) Model for Spatial Ability Development in STEM Students
proposals.motivation: The Belief, Skill, Knowledge (BSK) model is a promising framework for understanding and fostering spatial reasoning, a critical ability for STEM success. However, its development and validation have been largely confined to Western educational contexts. To determine if the BSK model is a universally applicable pedagogical tool, it must be tested across distinct cultural and national settings where educational practices, values, and prior experiences differ significantly.
proposals.hypothesis: The structural relationships between belief (self-efficacy), skill (spatial task performance), and knowledge (domain-specific conceptual understanding) within the BSK model are invariant across culturally and nationally diverse student populations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a large-scale, cross-sectional study with secondary and undergraduate STEM students from at least six countries (e.g., USA, Japan, Finland, South Africa, Brazil, UAE). Using validated, culturally adapted instruments, we will measure spatial self-efficacy (belief), performance on mental rotation and spatial visualization tasks (skill), and understanding of core spatial concepts (knowledge). Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) and Multi-Group Structural Equation Modeling (MG-SEM) will be employed to test for measurement invariance and compare the strength and significance of the BSK model's structural paths across groups.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide robust empirical evidence on the cultural generalizability of a key theoretical model in spatial education. It will directly inform the design of more equitable and effective international STEM outreach activities and curricula by identifying core, transferable elements versus context-specific adaptations needed for the BSK framework.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined cultural differences in spatial test scores, this study is the first to rigorously test the cross-cultural structural validity of the integrated BSK model itself, moving beyond mean comparisons to examine the fundamental relationships between its constructs.

proposals.title: Generalizing Computational and Creative Leadership (CCEL) Assessment: A Validation Study with In-Service Principals and Superintendents
proposals.motivation: The Computational and Creative Engineering Leadership (CCEL) instrument, developed and validated with graduate students, measures critical spatial reasoning and problem-solving abilities for STEM leadership. However, its generalizability to practicing school leaders—key agents in fostering K-12 STEM culture—remains untested. Validating the CCEL for this population is crucial to provide researchers and professional development designers with a reliable tool to assess and support leadership competencies directly tied to successful STEM education reform.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CCEL instrument will demonstrate strong construct validity and reliability when administered to in-service principals and superintendents, with performance patterns correlating meaningfully with their years of experience and self-reported engagement in STEM-focused school initiatives.
proposals.method: We will administer the established CCEL instrument, which uses scenario-based items and spatial manipulation tasks, to a stratified sample of approximately 150 in-service principals and superintendents. Data analysis will employ confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) to test the pre-defined factor structure (e.g., spatial visualization, systems reasoning). We will calculate internal consistency reliability (Cronbach's alpha) and conduct correlation analyses between CCEL subscale scores and demographic/experience variables collected via a supplementary survey.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence for the validity of the CCEL instrument among practicing school administrators, extending its utility beyond graduate education. It will contribute a validated assessment tool for research on leadership development and for evaluating the impact of STEM-focused professional development programs for school leaders.
proposals.novelty: While spatial and reasoning assessments exist in education, applying a leadership-focused computational creativity instrument, initially designed for engineers, to the distinct professional context of school administration is novel. This tests the transferability of a cognitive construct across professional domains.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Collective Creative Engineering Leadership (CCEL) on School Climate and Student STEM Achievement
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the Collective Creative Engineering Leadership (CCEL) framework has been used to assess collaborative problem-solving and leadership attributes in STEM outreach contexts, but its predictive validity for tangible educational outcomes remains unestablished. School climate and student achievement are critical, measurable indicators of educational effectiveness, yet the specific contribution of CCEL-aligned leadership factors is unknown. This gap limits the framework's utility for guiding evidence-based interventions in K-12 STEM education and outreach.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that higher scores on specific CCEL factors (e.g., collaborative innovation, systems reasoning) will show statistically significant positive correlations with improved school climate survey metrics and higher standardized student achievement scores in STEM subjects.
proposals.method: We will employ a correlational, multi-scale research design. First, we will administer the validated CCEL instrument to teacher and student leaders involved in pre-existing STEM outreach activities (e.g., Transportation Festivals). We will then link anonymized CCEL scores to two outcome datasets: (1) school-wide climate survey scores (e.g., from state or district surveys) and (2) aggregated, grade-level standardized test scores in mathematics and science. Analysis will use hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) to account for nested data structures, with Pearson and partial correlations computed to isolate relationships between specific CCEL factors and outcome variables while controlling for demographic covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence linking the CCEL framework to concrete educational outcomes, moving it from a descriptive tool to a predictive one. It will identify which leadership attributes most strongly associate with positive school environments and student achievement, offering actionable insights for designing targeted professional development and structuring engineer-educator collaborations in K-12 outreach.
proposals.novelty: While prior work used CCEL to assess attributes within specific outreach activities, this proposal innovatively connects these micro-scale assessments to macro-scale, longitudinal institutional outcomes (climate and achievement), a linkage not previously attempted. It also integrates disparate data sources (psychometric surveys, climate data, achievement scores) using HLM, a novel analytical approach for this research stream.

proposals.title: Identifying Enabling Conditions for the Transfer Boost: A Spatial-Ability-Informed Analysis of Post-Transfer GPA Improvement in STEM Students
proposals.motivation: Research on student transfer, particularly from community colleges to four-year institutions, often focuses on deficits and challenges, framing transfer as a risk factor for attrition or lower performance. This deficit-oriented perspective overlooks the significant subset of students who experience an academic 'boost'—a marked improvement in GPA—after transferring. Understanding the conditions that enable this positive outcome is crucial for designing supportive interventions and moving beyond a purely remedial view of transfer student support in STEM education.
proposals.hypothesis: Students who exhibit a post-transfer GPA improvement ('transfer boost') possess a distinct profile of spatial reasoning abilities and engage with specific, scaffolded research and outreach activities that mediate their successful academic acculturation and skill application in the new institutional environment.
proposals.method: The study will employ a longitudinal, mixed-methods design. We will recruit a cohort of STEM majors who have recently transferred and collect pre-/post-transfer GPA data, partitioning them into 'boost' and 'non-boost' groups. All participants will complete validated spatial ability assessments (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) and surveys on their engagement in high-impact practices (e.g., undergraduate research, STEM outreach festivals). We will use multivariate regression and propensity score matching to identify which combinations of spatial ability scores and activity attributes (frequency, mentorship quality) significantly predict membership in the 'boost' group. Follow-up interviews will provide qualitative depth on the enabling conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, strengths-based framework for understanding transfer student success, shifting the discourse from mitigation of risk to amplification of potential. It will provide empirical evidence linking specific cognitive abilities (spatial reasoning) and engagement modalities to positive post-transfer outcomes, offering actionable insights for academic advisors and program designers to cultivate a 'transfer boost' environment.
proposals.novelty: While spatial ability is known to predict general STEM success, its role as a potential lever for *post-transfer* academic improvement is unexplored. Furthermore, inverting the typical research question to study successful adapters, rather than strugglers, represents a significant conceptual novelty in transfer literature.

proposals.title: Gamifying Spatial Reasoning: A Framework for Enhancing Student Engagement and Adherence in STEM Learning Modules
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research shows that spatial ability is a strong predictor of success in STEM fields, yet interventions to develop it often suffer from low student engagement and adherence. Gamification, using points, badges, and leaderboards, has proven effective in educational contexts for increasing motivation and persistence in learning tasks. This project addresses the critical need to apply these techniques specifically to spatial reasoning training to improve completion rates and learning outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a structured gamification layer, with adaptive challenges and reward schedules, into a digital spatial reasoning curriculum will significantly increase student engagement, program adherence, and post-test spatial ability scores compared to a non-gamified version of the same curriculum.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based platform hosting validated spatial reasoning tasks (e.g., mental rotation, cross-sectioning). The experimental group will interact with a gamified version featuring: (1) a progression system with levels unlocking new task types, (2) a points and digital badge system for task completion and accuracy, and (3) a non-competitive leaderboard for self-comparison. Using a pre-post, control-group design with middle/high school students, we will measure adherence (completion time, task attempts) and spatial ability gains (using established instruments like the PSVT:R). Data will be analyzed using mixed-effects models to isolate the gamification effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, open-source framework for gamifying spatial learning, providing concrete design principles for educational technologists. It will produce empirical evidence on how specific game mechanics influence adherence and learning in a critical STEM aptitude domain, bridging educational psychology and learning engineering.
proposals.novelty: While gamification is common in general education apps, its targeted application to spatial ability training—a domain requiring sustained mental effort—is novel. The research integrates Lakin's expertise in spatial assessment scales with adaptive reward algorithms to create a personalized, scaffolded challenge system, moving beyond simple point accrual.

proposals.title: Trajectories of Spatial Self-Efficacy: Modeling Foundational Perceptions as Dynamic Predictors of STEM Engagement in Secondary Students
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in STEM education consistently identifies early perceptions, like self-efficacy and interest, as critical for long-term engagement. However, these perceptions are often treated as static traits or simple predictors, neglecting their dynamic interplay with educational experiences. A nuanced, longitudinal model of how specific spatial ability perceptions evolve and predict outcomes is needed to inform timely, effective interventions that can alter negative trajectories before they solidify.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that students' foundational perceptions of their spatial reasoning ability follow distinct developmental trajectories (e.g., stable, declining, or resilient) during middle and high school. Further, these trajectory classes will significantly predict later STEM course selection and performance, and will moderate the impact of targeted outreach activities (e.g., transportation festivals) on spatial skill gains.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-year longitudinal study tracking students from grades 6-10. Using validated spatial ability scales (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) and perception surveys (spatial self-efficacy, mindset), we will collect annual data. Trajectory analysis will employ Growth Mixture Modeling (GMM) in Mplus to identify latent classes of perception development. Predictive validity will be tested using hierarchical regression, controlling for covariates like prior achievement. The moderating effect of trajectory class on gains from engineered outreach events will be analyzed via multilevel modeling with cross-level interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a dynamic, person-centered model of how spatial perceptions develop, moving beyond variable-centered averages. It will empirically identify which students' perceptions are most formative versus malleable, offering a precise evidence base for staging interventions. The findings will directly inform the design of scalable K-12 outreach by revealing how to tailor activities to different perceptual trajectories.
proposals.novelty: This approach integrates Lakin's expertise in spatial assessment and STEM outreach with advanced longitudinal modeling techniques (GMM) not commonly applied to perceptual data in this domain. It reframes perceptions as time-varying processes with predictive power, rather than static traits, to test their role as dynamic moderators of intervention efficacy.

proposals.title: The Stability and Formative Role of First-Year Spatial Ability Perceptions in STEM Persistence: A Longitudinal, Multi-Scale Analysis
proposals.motivation: Retention in STEM fields remains a critical challenge, with early undergraduate experiences often cited as pivotal. Pre-2026 research on spatial ability, a key predictor of STEM success, has focused on static assessments but largely overlooked the dynamic nature of students' self-perceptions of this ability. Understanding whether initial self-perceptions are stable or malleable, and how they interact with later educational interventions, is essential for designing effective support structures.
proposals.hypothesis: First-year engineering students' perceptions of their own spatial ability are initially stable and formative, setting a cognitive framework that influences how they interpret and benefit from later, pivotal hands-on STEM outreach experiences, rather than being predictive of final ability or persistence on their own.
proposals.method: We will conduct a three-year longitudinal study tracking a cohort of first-year engineering students. We will administer validated spatial reasoning tests (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) and detailed perception surveys (capturing self-efficacy, mindset, and anxiety) at multiple time points. To analyze stability and formative influence, we will employ Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) to test path models linking initial perceptions, later participation in specific outreach activities (e.g., Transportation Festivals), and longitudinal outcomes (skill growth, persistence intent). Latent growth curve modeling will quantify perception change, and multi-group analysis will test for differential effects across student attributes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a nuanced, longitudinal model of how spatial ability perceptions develop and influence STEM pathways, moving beyond simple correlational claims. It will offer evidence-based guidance for the timing and design of K-12/University outreach collaborations by identifying which student perceptions are most formative and when interventions are most impactful.
proposals.novelty: While spatial ability is well-studied, this proposal uniquely treats students' *perceptions* of this ability as a stable, formative construct to be modeled longitudinally, explicitly testing their role as a filter for later experiences rather than a fixed predictor, which is a departure from dominant deficit-focused models.

proposals.title: Measuring Implicit Engineering Identity and Stereotypes in K-12 Students: A Multi-Method Validation Study
proposals.motivation: Explicit self-report surveys, the dominant tool for measuring STEM identity, are susceptible to social desirability bias and may not capture subconscious associations that influence student engagement and persistence. This is critical in K-12 outreach, where implicit stereotypes about who can be an engineer may undermine the efficacy of interventions. Grounded in pre-2026 literature on implicit social cognition in education, this study addresses the need for validated implicit measures to complement explicit data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that K-12 students' implicit associations between self-concepts and engineering, measured via a custom Implicit Association Test (IAT), will predict their engagement in spatial reasoning and design activities, even after controlling for explicit self-reported engineering identity. We further hypothesize that implicit stereotypes will vary significantly across demographic groups.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based Engineering Identity IAT using established JavaScript frameworks (e.g., jsPsych) where participants categorize stimuli representing 'Self'/'Other' and 'Engineering'/'Non-Engineering' concepts. Reaction time data will be processed using the D-algorithm standard in IAT research. This will be integrated with Lakin's existing spatial ability assessments (e.g., mental rotation, cross-sectioning) and explicit identity surveys. The study will involve a cross-sectional sample of middle and high school students participating in STEM Transportation Festival outreach events.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated implicit measure of engineering identity for K-12 populations, offering a more holistic assessment toolkit for researchers and educators. It will generate novel insights into how implicit biases interact with spatial reasoning—a core engineering ability—to influence student behavior in hands-on STEM activities, informing the design of more equitable and effective outreach.
proposals.novelty: While IATs are common in social psychology, their application to domain-specific identity in K-12 engineering education is novel. The integration of implicit measures with validated spatial ability scales and real-world outreach activities creates a unique multi-method framework not seen in prior STEM identity work.

proposals.title: Quantifying Identity and Cognitive-Affective States in Spatial Reasoning: A LIWC Analysis of Student Discourse
proposals.motivation: Research on spatial ability in STEM education often relies on performance metrics, neglecting the rich affective and identity-related discourse students produce during problem-solving. Understanding the emotional (e.g., anxiety, confidence) and cognitive (e.g., analytical thinking, insight) language linked to a student's identity as a 'spatial thinker' is crucial for designing interventions that support engagement and persistence, particularly for underrepresented groups. Automated text analysis offers a scalable method to move beyond traditional surveys and connect language patterns to spatial learning outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Student responses during spatial reasoning tasks will contain distinct linguistic markers, quantified by LIWC categories, that correlate with both their self-reported identity in STEM and their objective spatial ability scores. Specifically, higher use of analytical and insight words will correlate with stronger spatial performance, while affective language will be differentially linked to identity and engagement measures.
proposals.method: We will collect written and transcribed verbal responses from middle and high school students engaged in validated spatial ability tasks (e.g., mental rotation, perspective-taking) and outreach activities (e.g., from Transportation Festivals). Using the 2015 LIWC dictionary and software, we will computationally analyze text for pre-defined cognitive (e.g., insight, causation), affective (e.g., anxiety, confidence), and social (e.g., affiliation, identity) categories. Statistical analyses (correlation, regression, MANOVA) will link LIWC-derived scores to spatial test performance, pre/post identity surveys, and demographic attributes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for linking the linguistic content of student discourse to spatial learning and identity development. It will contribute a methodological advance to STEM education research by demonstrating how automated text analysis can uncover non-obvious cognitive-affective pathways in spatial reasoning. The findings will directly inform the design of more targeted and emotionally supportive K-12 STEM outreach and curriculum materials.
proposals.novelty: While LIWC is established in psychology, its application to analyze the intersection of spatial ability, identity, and affective states in authentic student STEM discourse is novel. This approach moves beyond assessing ability alone to quantify the concomitant linguistic expressions of thinking and feeling that shape a student's disciplinary identity.

proposals.title: Assessing the Impact of Normative Alignment on Student Transfer Success: A Spatial and Cognitive Analysis of Community College-University STEM Pathways
proposals.motivation: Articulation agreements between community colleges (CCs) and universities often focus on credit transfer but overlook the alignment of tacit academic norms (e.g., problem-solving approaches, research expectations, communication styles). This misalignment creates a hidden curriculum barrier, disproportionately impacting transfer students' sense of belonging and performance in STEM majors. Grounded in pre-2026 literature on transfer student equity and spatial reasoning as a predictor of STEM success, this research addresses a critical gap in creating seamless educational pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: Intentional alignment of specific academic norms (e.g., data representation standards, technical report frameworks, collaborative problem-solving protocols) between partner CC and university engineering programs will significantly improve transfer students' spatial reasoning performance, self-efficacy, and academic integration compared to a control group experiencing standard articulation.
proposals.method: The study employs a longitudinal, mixed-methods design with matched cohorts. We will develop and implement a 'Normative Alignment Intervention' comprising co-designed curricular modules and shared digital platforms for partner faculty. Student outcomes will be measured using pre-2026 validated instruments: (1) standardized spatial ability tests (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) to assess cognitive impact, (2) surveys on self-efficacy and belonging, and (3) analysis of project artifacts using a rubric co-developed with industry partners to assess normative uptake. Quantitative analysis will use hierarchical linear modeling to account for nested data.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, measurable framework for 'normative alignment' as a complement to credit transfer, directly extending Lakin's prior work on spatial ability and STEM outreach. It will yield evidence-based practices for engineer-educator collaborations focused on transfer pathways and provide a model for assessing non-content academic integration using cognitive and attitudinal scales.
proposals.novelty: While prior research identifies transfer challenges, this study uniquely operationalizes 'norms' as a malleable target for intervention and hypothesizes a direct, measurable effect on cognitive skills (spatial ability), moving beyond general retention studies. It integrates Lakin's expertise in spatial assessment with partnership development in a new, targeted way.

proposals.title: Decomposing Spatial Reasoning: A Cognitive Task Analysis of Metacognitive Demands in Guided vs. Independent STEM Learning
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research on spatial ability in STEM education often focuses on performance outcomes rather than the underlying cognitive processes required for independent learning. This gap is critical, as students must transition from structured, educator-led activities (like outreach festivals) to self-directed problem-solving. Understanding the specific metacognitive tasks—such as planning, monitoring, and strategy selection—in these different settings can inform better instructional scaffolds.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that independent spatial problem-solving tasks in STEM require a more explicit and complex set of metacognitive sub-tasks (e.g., error checking, mental rotation strategy selection) compared to guided, collaborative activities, and that these differences can be systematically mapped and measured.
proposals.method: We will employ a structured Cognitive Task Analysis (CTA) framework, specifically the Applied Cognitive Task Analysis (ACTA) protocol, to interview expert educators and analyze video data from two contrasting settings: 1) guided engineer-educator outreach activities, and 2) independent student work on validated spatial reasoning assessments. Interview and protocol data will be coded using a pre-2026 metacognitive coding schema (e.g., based on Flavell's or Schraw's frameworks) to identify and compare task sequences, decision points, and knowledge types. The analysis will produce detailed process maps for each setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a fine-grained, process-oriented model of the metacognitive demands inherent in spatial learning, moving beyond correlational studies of ability. It will provide empirical evidence to help educators and curriculum designers create targeted interventions that bridge the gap between supported outreach and independent student reasoning, particularly in K-12 STEM contexts.
proposals.novelty: While CTA is established in expert-novice studies, its application to compare the metacognitive structure of different educational *settings* (guided outreach vs. independent assessment) for spatial learning is novel. This integrates Lakin's work on spatial scales and outreach activities with cognitive science methods not typically used in engineering education research.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the Gatekeeper: A Multi-Method Analysis of Teacher Identification of Spatial and Reasoning Abilities in STEM Talent
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in STEM education consistently identifies teachers as primary gatekeepers for talent identification and advanced opportunities, yet this reliance may perpetuate inequities. Teacher judgments can be influenced by implicit biases related to gender, race, and classroom behavior, potentially overlooking students with high spatial or non-verbal reasoning abilities who do not conform to traditional academic profiles. This study directly investigates the validity of this foundational assumption to inform more equitable and scalable identification systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that teacher nominations for STEM talent will show low convergent validity with objective, performance-based measures of spatial and scientific reasoning ability, particularly for students from underrepresented groups. Teacher judgments will correlate more strongly with conventional academic attributes (e.g., grades, verbal participation) than with specific cognitive abilities predictive of STEM success.
proposals.method: The study employs a mixed-methods, multi-informant design. We will recruit middle/high school STEM teachers and their students. Students will complete validated, computer-based assessments of spatial ability (e.g., Mental Rotations Test, Paper Folding) and scientific reasoning (e.g., Lawson Test). We will develop and train a supervised machine learning model (e.g., Random Forest or Gradient Boosting) using student assessment scores, demographic data, and teacher-reported behavioral attributes as features to predict teacher nomination. The model's feature importance analysis will quantify which factors most drive teacher judgments. Concurrently, qualitative analysis of teacher interviews will explore their identification frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence quantifying the limitations of teacher-led talent identification, moving beyond anecdotal critique. It will contribute a novel methodological framework combining psychometrics, machine learning, and qualitative analysis to deconstruct identification processes. The findings will directly inform the design of more objective, multi-modal identification systems for K-12 outreach and talent development programs.
proposals.novelty: While bias in teacher judgments is studied, this project uniquely focuses on spatial and reasoning abilities—critical but often overlooked STEM predictors—and uses interpretable machine learning to dissect the 'black box' of teacher decision-making. It bridges Lakin's work on spatial ability assessment and STEM outreach by critically examining the primary human sensor in the current identification pipeline.

proposals.title: Assessing the Efficacy of Formal University Training Versus Informal Outreach in Teacher Recognition of Spatial Talent in STEM Students
proposals.motivation: Effective identification and nurturing of spatial talent in K-12 students is critical for developing future STEM innovators, yet it remains unclear how best to equip educators for this role. Pre-2026 literature highlights the importance of spatial ability for STEM success but shows a gap in teacher professional development, often relying on short-term outreach. This study directly addresses whether sustained, formal university-led training is superior to informal, activity-based outreach for building teacher competency in talent recognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that teachers who receive structured, university-certified training in spatial ability assessment and talent development frameworks will demonstrate significantly higher accuracy and confidence in identifying and supporting high-spatial-ability students compared to teachers who only participate in informal, project-based STEM outreach activities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental, mixed-methods study comparing two groups of middle/high school STEM teachers (N=60). The intervention group will complete a semester-long university micro-credential course covering spatial reasoning theory, validated assessment tools (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT), and differentiated instructional strategies. The comparison group will participate in an equivalent duration of engaging, informal outreach activities (e.g., transportation festivals, engineer collaborations). Pre/post, we will measure outcomes using: 1) a validated instrument assessing teachers' knowledge of spatial constructs and talent attributes, 2) analysis of teacher evaluations of student work samples against expert-rated benchmarks, and 3) surveys on teacher self-efficacy. Data will be analyzed using ANCOVA and thematic analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for designing effective teacher professional development programs focused on spatial talent identification. It will contribute a scalable model for university-school partnerships that moves beyond engagement to build deep pedagogical content knowledge. The findings will inform policy on credentialing for talent development specialists in K-12 STEM education.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Lakin and others has developed engaging outreach activities and tools for assessing spatial ability, this study uniquely isolates and tests the formal training component as a necessary condition for effective teacher practice, moving from measuring student ability to building teacher diagnostic skill.

proposals.title: Cultivating Spatial Reasoning: A Dynamic, Activity-Based Assessment Framework for Measuring Growth in STEM Talent
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 STEM education and talent identification often rely on static assessments that treat spatial ability as a fixed, innate trait, potentially overlooking students whose potential can be developed through targeted experiences. This 'fixed talent' paradigm can limit equity and diversity in STEM pathways by prematurely filtering out students. Shifting the focus from discovery to cultivation is crucial for broadening participation and supporting a growth mindset in K-12 education.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic assessment framework, integrating scaffolded learning activities with repeated spatial reasoning measures, will reveal significant, measurable growth in students' spatial abilities, challenging the notion of spatial talent as a fixed trait. This growth will be more pronounced and equitable compared to outcomes from single-timepoint, high-stakes testing.
proposals.method: We will design a longitudinal study with middle school students, employing a pre-post-intervention design. The intervention consists of a series of hands-on, embodied spatial reasoning activities (e.g., mental rotation with physical manipulatives, sketching from multiple perspectives) adapted from proven STEM outreach models. Growth will be measured using a multi-scale assessment battery: validated psychometric tests (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) for broad ability, and fine-grained analysis of solution pathways and reasoning artifacts (sketches, verbal protocols) coded for specific attributes like perspective-taking and mental transformation. Analysis will use latent growth curve modeling to quantify change trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, process-oriented assessment methodology that quantifies the *cultivation* of spatial talent, moving beyond static snapshots. It will provide empirical evidence for a growth-based model of STEM talent development, offering educators and researchers tools to design and evaluate inclusive, ability-enhancing curricula. The findings will directly inform more equitable practices in STEM outreach and talent identification.
proposals.novelty: While spatial training studies exist, this proposal uniquely integrates dynamic assessment principles—blending instruction and measurement—with multi-scale analysis of both general ability and specific cognitive attributes to model growth, a significant departure from fixed-trait discovery paradigms in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Framework for Assessing and Enhancing Spatial Reasoning in K-12 Students Through Structured Industry-Educator Mentorship Networks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research consistently shows that spatial ability is a strong predictor of success in STEM fields, yet it is often underdeveloped in standard K-12 curricula. Concurrently, calls for authentic, community-engaged learning highlight a gap in scalable models that formally connect teacher professional development with sustained industry mentorship, limiting students' exposure to applied spatial reasoning and career pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a structured mentorship network, which pairs teachers trained in spatial ability interventions with local industry experts, will lead to measurable gains in students' spatial reasoning scales and increase their STEM career identity compared to classroom-only interventions.
proposals.method: The study will employ a quasi-experimental, mixed-methods design. Teacher-industry pairs will co-develop and implement project-based activities (e.g., transportation system design) targeting specific spatial attributes. Student outcomes will be assessed using validated spatial reasoning instruments (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) and surveys on STEM attitudes. Data analysis will use hierarchical linear modeling to account for nested student data and thematic analysis of mentorship logs and interviews to identify effective partnership attributes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated framework for building sustainable STEM community partnerships focused on cognitive skill development. It will provide empirical evidence on how specific mentorship structures influence spatial learning outcomes and produce a toolkit of co-designed activities linking spatial reasoning to real-world engineering problems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores outreach events or teacher training in isolation, this study's novelty lies in formally integrating these components into a continuous mentorship network and directly measuring its impact on a foundational cognitive ability (spatial reasoning) rather than general engagement alone.

proposals.title: Spatial Ability Development in Urban and Suburban Under-Resourced Schools: Adapting and Validating a STEM Outreach Model
proposals.motivation: Prior research on spatial ability development and STEM outreach has often focused on rural or specific underserved contexts, leaving a gap in validated models for urban and suburban Title I schools. These non-rural settings present distinct challenges, such as high student density, diverse linguistic backgrounds, and different community resource structures, which may impact the efficacy of established engagement principles. Adapting proven models is crucial for equitable STEM education and broadening the pipeline of future engineers.
proposals.hypothesis: The core principles of the collaborative engineer-educator outreach model, which successfully engaged students in rural/festival settings, can be effectively adapted to improve spatial reasoning and STEM identity among middle school students in urban and suburban under-resourced schools. We hypothesize that adaptation will require contextual modifications to activities and scales but will retain a significant positive impact on targeted student attributes.
proposals.method: We will adapt existing outreach activities (e.g., from Transportation Festivals) for classroom implementation in a quasi-experimental design across 10-12 urban/suburban Title I schools. The intervention's impact will be assessed using a pre/post-test design with validated spatial ability scales (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) and surveys measuring STEM engagement and self-efficacy. Data analysis will employ hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) to account for nested student data and compare outcomes with prior rural/festival data, using ANOVA and regression techniques to identify significant predictors of spatial gains in the new context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, context-adapted framework for spatial learning and STEM outreach specifically designed for non-rural underserved schools. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of collaborative outreach models and produce a set of refined, scalable activities and assessment protocols for researchers and practitioners working in similar settings.
proposals.novelty: While spatial training and outreach models exist, systematically testing and adapting a researcher-engineer-educator collaboration model for the specific socio-environmental constraints of urban/suburban under-resourced schools is novel. It moves beyond a one-size-fits-all approach to equity in STEM education.

proposals.title: A Talent Identification Framework for STEM Potential: Translating Elite Sports Methodologies to Rural Youth Populations
proposals.motivation: Rural students face significant barriers to STEM engagement and are often overlooked by traditional talent identification programs, which rely on academic proxies and urban-centric opportunities. This represents a critical equity gap and a loss of potential innovation. Pre-2026 research in elite sports has developed robust, multi-dimensional frameworks for identifying athletic potential based on physical, cognitive, and psychological attributes, offering a model for a more holistic and equitable approach.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic framework, adapted from elite sports talent identification, can effectively identify latent STEM potential in rural youth by assessing a composite of spatial reasoning, problem-solving attributes, and contextual engagement factors that are not captured by standard academic metrics.
proposals.method: We will adapt the multi-tiered talent identification model used in sports (e.g., combining general screening with focused diagnostic testing) to a STEM context. The framework will integrate: 1) A scalable screening phase using validated, game-based digital activities to assess general spatial ability and reasoning attributes. 2) A diagnostic phase employing higher-order assessment tasks, informed by Lakin's prior work on LLM-aided evaluation, to measure specific cognitive dimensions like mental rotation and structural visualization. 3) Contextual data on student engagement in STEM-related activities and projects, analogous to tracking athletic training history.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, operational framework for equitable STEM talent identification, specifically designed for underserved rural populations. It will advance the field of spatial cognition and STEM education research by providing validated, multi-scale assessment protocols that link cognitive attributes to real-world problem-solving potential, moving beyond grade-based identification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of a structured, attribute-based identification paradigm from elite sports—a domain focused on latent potential—to the challenge of spotting STEM talent, particularly in geographic contexts where potential is most likely to be masked by lack of opportunity.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Spatial Talent Development Pathways: A Community-Based Participatory Research Framework for Rural STEM Equity
proposals.motivation: Rural students possess significant, often untapped spatial reasoning abilities critical for STEM success, but traditional talent development models are frequently designed by external researchers without deep community context, risking misalignment with local values, assets, and needs. This disconnect can perpetuate inequities in STEM participation and fail to leverage unique rural spatial learning environments (e.g., agricultural, mechanical, natural landscapes). Grounding development in Community-Based Participatory Research (CBPR) principles, as advocated in pre-2026 public health and education literature, offers a transformative approach to create culturally sustaining STEM pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: That a CBPR framework, co-designing spatial ability assessments and talent development activities with rural community stakeholders (educators, parents, industry partners, students), will yield more ecologically valid, engaging, and sustainable interventions than researcher-driven models, as measured by participant engagement, perceived relevance, and spatial reasoning gains.
proposals.method: The project will employ a multi-phase, mixed-methods CBPR design. First, a community advisory board (CAB) of rural stakeholders will be established to guide all research phases. Using participatory workshops and design thinking activities, the CAB will co-define 'spatial talent' in their context and co-design culturally relevant assessment items, adapting validated instruments like the Purdue Spatial Visualization Tests. Subsequently, the team and CAB will co-develop a suite of hands-on, place-based STEM activities (e.g., related to local transportation, agriculture, or infrastructure) targeting identified spatial constructs. Pre/post assessments using the co-created measures and qualitative interviews will evaluate the intervention's impact.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, transferable CBPR protocol for equitable STEM talent development research, specifically for rural settings. It will produce new, community-validated instruments for assessing spatial ability and a toolkit of co-designed, place-based activities that bridge research on spatial reasoning with practical, community-engaged outreach, directly extending the PI's work on STEM festivals and educator collaborations.
proposals.novelty: While CBPR is established in other fields, its systematic application to the cognitive construct of spatial ability development and STEM talent identification in rural education is novel. It moves beyond delivering pre-packaged outreach to building endogenous, community-owned research and development capacity for nurturing STEM talent.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Locally Relevant STEM Challenges: A Design Thinking Framework for Enhancing Spatial Reasoning and Engagement in K-12 Students
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in STEM education highlights persistent gaps in student engagement and spatial ability development, particularly for underrepresented groups. Existing curricula often lack local relevance, which is crucial for contextual learning and sustained interest. This project addresses the need for scalable, participatory methods to create authentic, place-based learning experiences that bridge research and classroom practice.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured design thinking workshop model for teacher-student co-creation of locally relevant STEM challenges will lead to measurable improvements in student spatial reasoning skills and increased engagement with STEM activities, compared to standard curriculum delivery.
proposals.method: We will conduct iterative design thinking workshops (empathize, define, ideate, prototype, test) with middle/high school teachers and students to co-create STEM challenges focused on local spatial problems (e.g., urban mobility, infrastructure). We will employ a mixed-methods approach, using validated spatial ability scales (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) for pre/post assessment and thematic analysis of workshop artifacts and interviews. The intervention's impact will be evaluated through a quasi-experimental design comparing co-creation classrooms with control groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical framework for curriculum co-creation that integrates design thinking into K-12 STEM education. It will generate empirical evidence on the impact of locally relevant, co-designed challenges on specific cognitive attributes like spatial reasoning. The resulting model will provide a template for sustainable engineer-educator collaborations in outreach.
proposals.novelty: While design thinking is used in education, its systematic application for *joint* teacher-student curriculum co-creation targeting *spatial ability* through *locally anchored* challenges is novel. This integrates Lakin's prior work on spatial assessment and STEM outreach into a unified, participatory action research model.

proposals.title: Empowering Student Talent Scouts: A Peer-Recognition Intervention to Identify and Nurture Latent STEM Abilities in Middle School Classrooms
proposals.motivation: Traditional STEM talent identification often relies on top-down assessments by teachers or standardized tests, which can miss latent or non-traditional abilities, particularly in underrepresented groups. Empowering students to recognize STEM talent in their peers leverages social dynamics and observational learning, potentially creating a more equitable and supportive classroom ecosystem that fosters a growth mindset and broadens participation in STEM pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: Middle school students, when provided with structured training on the observable attributes of STEM reasoning and spatial ability, can reliably identify these competencies in their peers, and this peer-recognition process will increase the identified students' self-efficacy and engagement in STEM activities.
proposals.method: We will develop and test a multi-week intervention for middle school classrooms. The intervention includes: 1) Training modules where students analyze video vignettes of peer problem-solving in STEM activities (e.g., engineering design challenges) and learn to code for specific, pre-2026 established attributes like spatial manipulation, systems reasoning, and iterative testing. 2) Implementation of a structured peer-nomination system where students use a validated digital rubric to nominate peers demonstrating these attributes during collaborative projects. 3) Analysis using pre-post surveys of self-efficacy and engagement, alongside network analysis of nomination patterns and correlation with traditional spatial ability assessments (e.g., PSVT:R) to validate peer judgments.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, student-centered framework for talent identification that complements educator assessment. It will produce a validated training protocol and instrument for peer recognition, adding to the literature on equity in STEM education by decentralizing the 'expert' gaze and leveraging peer social networks to surface latent talent.
proposals.novelty: While prior research assesses student abilities, this proposal inverts the model, treating students as assessment agents. It uniquely integrates training on specific cognitive attributes (spatial, reasoning) with structured peer-nomination methodologies from social network analysis, moving beyond simple peer feedback to formal talent scouting.

proposals.title: Beyond the National Stage: Quantifying the Motivational and Attitudinal Impact of Regional STEM Fair Participation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on STEM competitions often assumes that progression to higher, more selective levels (e.g., state, national) is the primary mechanism for fostering positive student attitudes and self-efficacy. This focus may overlook the significant motivational and developmental value of the initial, accessible regional fair experience. A more nuanced understanding is needed to design equitable outreach that benefits all participants, not just the top-tier winners.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a majority of middle and high school participants, the experience of preparing for and presenting at a local or regional STEM fair is a more significant driver of positive attitude gains in science and engineering than the prospect of advancing to a higher competition level.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, multi-scale survey study of students participating in a regional STEM transportation festival. Using validated pre- and post-event instruments (e.g., modified S-STEM surveys), we will measure shifts in attitudes, self-efficacy, and spatial reasoning. We will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) to analyze the relationships between participation attributes (e.g., quality of mentorship, project type), perceived success at the regional fair, and attitude gains, while controlling for the influence of advancement to higher competitions. This will isolate the specific contribution of the regional fair experience.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the intrinsic value of regional STEM fairs, shifting the narrative from pure competition to inclusive engagement. It will offer organizers and educators a data-driven framework to optimize event design for broad student development, supporting the goals of K12 outreach and engineer-educator collaborations.
proposals.novelty: This study directly challenges the implicit hierarchy in competition research by testing whether the foundational, accessible tier of participation is itself the key motivational engine, a non-obvious claim given the field's emphasis on elite advancement pathways.

proposals.title: Decomposing the Impact: A Causal Analysis of Program Components on Teacher and Student Engagement in STEM Outreach Collaborations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on K-12 STEM outreach emphasizes the importance of teacher buy-in and student participation for program success, but often treats support programs as monolithic interventions. This makes it difficult for resource-limited institutions and researchers to efficiently design and scale effective outreach. A granular understanding of which specific program components (e.g., material provision, co-planning time, engineer classroom visits) drive engagement is critically needed to optimize collaborative models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, measurable program components related to logistical support and collaborative professional development will have a significantly stronger causal relationship with teacher buy-in and subsequent student participation than broader, less-defined program attributes.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, quasi-experimental design. First, we will decompose existing outreach program data (e.g., from transportation festivals and educator collaborations) into discrete, coded components using a structured rubric. We will then apply causal forest algorithms, an ensemble machine learning method based on decision trees, to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects of these components on engagement metrics (teacher surveys, student participation rates). This will be supplemented by qualitative analysis of educator interviews to contextualize the quantitative findings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, component-level framework for analyzing STEM outreach programs, moving beyond general program evaluation. It will offer evidence-based guidelines for practitioners to prioritize the most impactful support elements. For researchers, it introduces a causal inference approach to the study of educational collaborations, enabling more precise hypothesis testing in future work.
proposals.novelty: While program evaluation is common, applying causal machine learning techniques (causal forests) to isolate the effects of individual program components within educator-engineer collaborations is a novel methodological advance in STEM education research. This moves from correlation to a more robust estimation of what specifically drives engagement.

proposals.title: Co-Designing for Equity: A Human-Centered Framework for Teacher Support Resources in Spatial and STEM Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Teacher support programs, especially in STEM and spatial reasoning, often fail due to a top-down design that misaligns with classroom realities, leading to low adoption and inequitable outcomes. Pre-2026 research highlights the importance of teacher buy-in and contextual fit for effective implementation, yet systematic methods for integrating teacher voice into resource design remain underdeveloped. This gap is critical in spatial ability development, where equitable access for diverse student populations depends on scalable, teacher-validated supports.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a structured, multi-phase design thinking process to co-create teacher support resources will yield materials with higher perceived utility, contextual fit, and intended adoption rates compared to resources developed through expert-driven design alone.
proposals.method: The study will employ a three-phase, mixed-methods design thinking protocol (Empathize, Ideate, Prototype) with K-12 STEM educators. In the Empathize phase, contextual inquiry and journey mapping will identify pain points in implementing spatial reasoning activities. The Ideate phase will use facilitated co-design workshops with affinity diagramming to generate resource concepts. Finally, iterative prototyping and testing cycles will employ A/B testing of resource formats (e.g., visual scaffolds vs. scripted dialogues) and measure outcomes via validated scales for perceived usefulness, ease of use (adapted from TAM), and stated adoption intent.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a replicable, human-centered design framework specifically for educational resource development, documented with case studies. It will produce a set of co-designed, empirically tested support resources for spatial reasoning instruction, directly addressing the researcher's interest in scales and student abilities. The findings will advance knowledge on the mechanisms linking co-design processes to educator adoption and program sustainability.
proposals.novelty: While design thinking is used in product development, its systematic application to the granular problem of teacher support resource creation—particularly for spatial ability—integrates educational research with human-centered design methods in a novel way. The proposal moves beyond general outreach collaborations to engineer a structured process for equitable resource co-creation.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Spatial Reasoning Skill Persistence and Transfer in K-12 STEM Outreach Programs
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on STEM outreach and spatial ability training often focuses on immediate post-test gains, leaving a critical gap in understanding the long-term retention and real-world application of these skills. For educators and researchers like Lakin, who design interventions (e.g., Transportation Festivals), it is essential to know if and how acquired skills persist and are functionally applied by students over time, beyond the program's duration. This knowledge is vital for moving from measuring simple engagement to evaluating the sustained impact of educational investments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the persistence and application of spatial reasoning skills acquired through targeted STEM outreach activities will vary significantly across different student ability levels and will be moderated by specific contextual factors, such as subsequent STEM course enrollment and self-efficacy. The functional transfer of skills will be more strongly predicted by these longitudinal moderators than by initial training intensity alone.
proposals.method: This study employs a 24-month longitudinal, mixed-methods design with middle/high school participants from engineer-educator collaboration events. We will administer the revised Purdue Spatial Visualization Tests (PSVT:R) and custom surveys at multiple timepoints (pre, post, 6, 12, 24 months) to track quantitative skill decay. Qualitative data from semi-structured interviews will explore barriers and facilitators to skill application. Analysis will use latent growth curve modeling to map skill trajectories and thematic analysis of interview transcripts, integrating findings to build a comprehensive model of effect persistence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, multi-dimensional framework for assessing the long-term efficacy of spatial and STEM training programs, moving beyond enrollment metrics. It will produce actionable insights for researchers and practitioners on designing interventions with durable impacts, specifically within the context of K-12 outreach collaborations. The findings will contribute directly to the literature on spatial ability development and the evaluation of informal STEM education.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a rigorous longitudinal, mixed-methods assessment specifically to the domain of spatial ability training within real-world K-12 outreach settings, an area typically studied with short-term, quantitative-only designs. It uniquely integrates Lakin's focus on spatial scales, student attributes, and collaborative outreach to investigate the dynamic interplay between skill retention and contextual application over an extended period.

proposals.title: A Multi-State, Multi-Platform Evaluation of a Scalable Training Program for Enhancing Spatial Reasoning in K-12 STEM Education
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research consistently identifies spatial ability as a critical predictor for success in STEM fields, yet scalable interventions to develop this skill in diverse student populations remain limited. The IIS (Integrating Industry and STEM) training program, which uses collaborative, activity-based modules, has shown promise in pilot studies but requires rigorous evaluation across varied educational contexts and technological platforms to establish its generalizability and scalability for national impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core pedagogical principles of the IIS training program can be effectively implemented across multiple states using different IIS technological platforms, and that this scaled implementation will lead to measurable, significant gains in students' comprehensive spatial reasoning abilities, independent of specific platform attributes.
proposals.method: This study employs a quasi-experimental, mixed-methods design across 3-5 states, each utilizing a different IIS platform (e.g., web-based, mobile, VR-lite). We will adapt the core training modules for platform consistency while preserving pedagogical activities. Student spatial ability will be assessed pre- and post-intervention using a validated, multi-scale instrument combining psychometric tests (e.g., Mental Rotation, Spatial Visualization) and higher-order performance tasks analyzed via established rubric-based reasoning frameworks. Implementation fidelity, educator feedback, and platform usability will be concurrently evaluated through surveys, interviews, and activity logs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, evidence-based framework for scaling effective spatial training programs nationally, directly addressing the research-to-practice gap in STEM education. It will yield critical insights into how program efficacy interacts with technological infrastructure and regional educational policies, offering practical guidelines for researcher-educator-industry collaborations aimed at broad outreach.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has evaluated spatial training in isolated contexts, this study is novel in its systematic, comparative investigation of scaling an integrated industry-education program across heterogeneous state systems and technological implementations, moving beyond proof-of-concept to actionable scaling knowledge.

proposals.title: Mapping Systemic and Instructional Assets: A Computational Framework to Predict and Enhance Spatial Learning in K-12 STEM
proposals.motivation: Asset-based frameworks in STEM education have primarily focused on identifying and leveraging student traits, such as prior spatial ability. However, this individual-centric view overlooks the powerful role of system-level factors, such as curriculum design, teacher facilitation, and resource availability, which are critical for equitable learning. Grounded in pre-2026 research on spatial cognition and STEM outreach, this proposal addresses the need to systematically model these often-invisible systemic assets to better understand and engineer effective learning environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational model integrating quantifiable systemic and instructional assets (e.g., activity structure, material affordances, collaborative protocols) will more accurately predict student gains in spatial reasoning than models based solely on pre-existing student attributes. Furthermore, we hypothesize that this model will reveal specific, actionable asset configurations that optimize learning outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale computational framework. First, we will codify systemic assets from video and artifact data of existing STEM outreach activities (e.g., Transportation Festivals) using a structured ontology of instructional design features. Second, we will employ graph neural networks (GNNs) to model the relationships between these system assets, student pre-test attributes, and post-test spatial ability scores. The GNN architecture will treat assets and students as nodes in a heterogeneous graph, learning to propagate influence and predict outcomes. Validation will use pre-2026 datasets from researcher-led outreach events.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable methodology for asset mapping in educational systems, moving beyond student deficits. It will produce an evidence-based taxonomy of high-leverage instructional assets for spatial learning. The resulting predictive model will provide educators and curriculum designers with a tool to audit and intentionally design learning environments for greater efficacy and equity.
proposals.novelty: While asset-based approaches exist, applying computational graph-based modeling to explicitly invert the focus from student traits to systemic and instructional features as the primary explanatory nodes is a novel shift. This methodologically advances the field by operationalizing often qualitative system characteristics into a testable, predictive framework.

proposals.title: Universal Challenge as an Equity Mechanism: Testing the Redundancy of Gifted Identification in Spatial Reasoning Tasks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, equity in STEM education often focuses on identifying and serving students with high abilities, particularly in domains like spatial reasoning. This identification process can be resource-intensive, biased, and may inadvertently create fixed-mindset environments. This research directly challenges the foundational assumption that identification is a prerequisite for equitable access to advanced learning, proposing instead that universally available, high-quality challenge may render identification unnecessary.
proposals.hypothesis: When all students are provided with universally designed, high-challenge spatial reasoning activities, the variance in performance and growth attributable to prior gifted identification will be statistically non-significant, and student engagement will be high across diverse ability levels.
proposals.method: We will design a quasi-experimental study in middle school STEM classrooms, using a series of spatially demanding engineering design tasks (e.g., 3D modeling, transportation system optimization) as the universal high-challenge intervention. Student spatial ability will be assessed pre- and post-intervention using a validated instrument like the Purdue Spatial Visualization Test: Rotations (PSVT:R). We will employ a mixed-effects regression model, with growth in spatial scores as the dependent variable and prior gifted identification as a fixed effect, while controlling for covariates like prior STEM experience. Engagement will be measured via validated survey scales and analysis of task persistence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on whether a 'first-principles' approach to equity—providing high-quality challenge to all—can effectively meet the needs of high-ability learners without formal gatekeeping. It will contribute a practical framework for 'de-labeling' advanced STEM instruction, aligning with broader pre-2026 movements towards inclusive, heterogeneous grouping and mastery learning.
proposals.novelty: While universal design and differentiation are established concepts, directly testing the redundancy of gifted identification as a system is novel. It merges the researcher's expertise in spatial assessment and STEM outreach into a focused experimental test of a core sociological assumption in ability grouping.

proposals.title: Operationalizing an Asset-Based Decision Framework: A Data-Driven Intervention for Enhancing Spatial Ability Development in K-12 STEM
proposals.motivation: Educational decision-making, particularly in K-12 STEM, has historically been deficit-focused, identifying and remediating student weaknesses. This approach can demotivate learners and overlook critical strengths. An asset-based framework, which identifies and leverages existing student and community resources (e.g., prior reasoning skills, spatial experiences, local industry partnerships), offers a promising alternative but lacks operational, data-driven models for implementation in real school settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a formalized, data-driven asset-based decision model in school outreach and curriculum design will lead to measurable improvements in student engagement and spatial reasoning performance compared to traditional, deficit-oriented approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational decision-support system that integrates multi-source data (pre-assessments of spatial ability scales, student interest surveys, teacher logs of classroom activities). Using a Bayesian network architecture, the model will infer latent student 'asset profiles' (e.g., strengths in mental rotation vs. navigation). These profiles will then inform the adaptive selection of targeted STEM outreach activities (e.g., transportation festival modules) designed to build on identified assets. The system will be deployed in a quasi-experimental study across multiple middle schools.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically tested, computational model for asset-based educational decision-making. It will contribute a novel methodology for translating the theoretical asset-based framework into actionable tools for educators and researchers, specifically within the domain of spatial ability and STEM engagement. The findings will offer concrete evidence on the efficacy of asset-based versus deficit-based interventions.
proposals.novelty: While asset-based pedagogies are discussed in literature, their operationalization through a formal data-driven decision model, particularly one integrating Bayesian inference for student profiling to tailor STEM outreach, is novel. This bridges educational theory with computational analytics in a way not previously demonstrated in pre-2026 work on spatial ability and K-12 outreach.

proposals.title: A Data-Driven Framework for Tiered Advancement in STEM: Applying RTI Decision Rules to Spatial Ability Development
proposals.motivation: Response to Intervention (RTI) is a well-established, data-driven framework for identifying and supporting K-12 students with learning difficulties, but its structured decision rules for tier movement are rarely applied to advanced or gifted education. This creates a gap in systematically identifying and nurturing students with high potential in specific STEM domains, such as spatial reasoning, which is a critical predictor of success in engineering and physical sciences. A formalized, scalable method for moving students between tiers of advanced instruction is needed to optimize talent development.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying and adapting core RTI decision rules (e.g., benchmark screening, progress monitoring with trend lines, dual discrepancy models for rate and level of performance) to spatial ability instruction will create a valid and reliable system for making data-informed decisions about student movement between tiers of advanced STEM enrichment.
proposals.method: The study will develop and test a three-tiered intervention model for middle/high school students. Tier 1 involves whole-class spatial visualization training. Tier 2 provides small-group, targeted enrichment for students not meeting pre-set growth benchmarks on instruments like the Purdue Spatial Visualization Test: Rotations (PSVT:R). Tier 3 offers intensive, individualized projects. Movement between tiers will be governed by algorithmic decision rules implemented in a dashboard, using CUSUM control charts to monitor individual progress and a dual-discrepancy criterion (comparing a student's performance and growth rate to a local norm group) to trigger tier changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, operational framework for talent development that translates special education methodology to gifted/advanced STEM education. It will produce a validated set of quantitative decision rules and a prototype dashboard tool for educators, moving beyond subjective nomination for advanced opportunities. The research will also provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of tiered, differentiated instruction for developing spatial ability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic transfer of RTI's data-based decision-making architecture—a paradigm for remediation—to the domain of advanced academic talent development, specifically for a malleable cognitive skill (spatial ability) critical to STEM. This cross-paradigm application to create a dynamic, responsive system for advancement is distinct from static gifted identification models.

proposals.title: Dynamic Assessment of Spatial Learning Potential in STEM Students: A Test of Mediation and Transfer
proposals.motivation: Static assessments of spatial ability, while predictive of STEM success, fail to capture a student's capacity to learn with targeted support, potentially overlooking individuals with high learning potential.
