Grounded in Vygotskian theory, dynamic assessment (DA) measures the zone of proximal development by evaluating performance change following guided instruction, offering a more equitable and informative metric for educational intervention. This approach aligns with the need for scalable, research-based methods to identify and nurture spatial reasoning in diverse K-12 and undergraduate populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dynamic assessment protocol, featuring graduated mediator prompts during spatial reasoning tasks, will reveal significant variability in learning potential not captured by static pre-tests. Furthermore, gains from this mediated learning session will show stronger transfer to novel STEM problem-solving activities than static ability scores.
proposals.method: We will develop a digital, adaptive dynamic assessment module for mental rotation and spatial visualization. The module will employ a test-mediate-retest design, using a rule-based algorithm to deliver standardized, increasingly explicit hint prompts (mediation) when a student struggles. The system will log all interactions, calculating a learning potential score based on the reduction in hints needed and improvement in accuracy between isomorphic pre- and post-mediation tasks. This will be piloted with middle/high school students participating in STEM transportation outreach festivals, comparing DA outcomes to static spatial tests and performance on a subsequent engineering design challenge.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, scalable protocol for the dynamic assessment of spatial learning potential, moving the field beyond static benchmarking. It will provide empirical evidence on how mediated learning gains predict STEM task transfer, offering educators a tool to tailor interventions based on a student's responsiveness to instruction rather than innate ability alone.
proposals.novelty: While dynamic assessment is established in educational psychology, its application to spatial ability measurement in informal STEM learning contexts is novel. Integrating this methodology with technology-enhanced, scalable mediation protocols tailored for spatial tasks represents a significant advance over traditional paper-based or one-on-one DA approaches.

proposals.title: An Asset-Based Decision Support Tool for Daily, Flexible Student Grouping in STEM Classrooms
proposals.motivation: Effective small-group work in K-12 STEM education is critical for engagement and conceptual reasoning, but teachers lack practical, scalable tools to form groups based on multidimensional student assets beyond simple academic ability. Existing grouping research often operates at coarse scales (e.g., semester-level interventions) or relies on static, standardized test data, failing to support the dynamic, daily decision-making required in authentic classrooms. This gap limits the personalization and responsiveness of collaborative learning activities.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a dynamic, asset-based grouping framework that integrates real-time, multi-attribute student data (e.g., spatial reasoning, prior knowledge, communication style) will enable teachers to form more effective flexible groups, leading to measurable improvements in student engagement and collaborative problem-solving performance during in-class STEM activities.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based decision support tool for teachers. The tool will use a multi-attribute utility algorithm, drawing on pre-2026 research in psychometrics and learning analytics, to process daily input on student attributes (e.g., via brief pre-activity surveys or past performance rubrics). The algorithm will optimize group composition for heterogeneity or homogeneity based on teacher-selected goals (e.g., balancing spatial reasoning skills for a design task). We will conduct a mixed-methods study in middle/high school STEM classrooms, using teacher interviews, classroom observation protocols, and pre/post assessments of student spatial ability and attitudes to evaluate the tool's usability and impact.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical instrument for classroom-level, data-informed pedagogical decision-making, directly scaling down asset-based frameworks from theoretical research to daily practice. It will generate empirical evidence on how dynamic grouping based on a broader set of cognitive and non-cognitive attributes affects collaborative STEM learning processes and outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While asset-based frameworks exist, their operationalization for *daily* teacher-led grouping decisions using a dynamic, multi-attribute algorithmic support tool is novel. This bridges the gap between large-scale educational data mining and the immediate, contextual needs of individual teachers, a scale largely unaddressed in pre-2026 learning analytics research.

proposals.title: Broadening the Pipeline: Evaluating the Impact of Universal STEM Enrichment on Spatial Ability Development and Talent Identification
proposals.motivation: Traditional STEM talent identification, especially in spatial reasoning, often relies on high-stakes testing and early screening, which can be biased and miss latent potential. This approach may inadvertently exclude students from underrepresented groups who lack prior exposure. Shifting the paradigm from precise identification to universal opportunity provision could create a more equitable and robust STEM pipeline.
proposals.hypothesis: Providing broad, engaging STEM enrichment activities to a diverse middle school population will develop spatial reasoning abilities across the cohort, thereby reducing the predictive necessity of pre-intervention identification measures for spotting STEM talent.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental study in middle schools, implementing a semester-long, multi-modal STEM enrichment festival (integrating physical modeling, VR spatial tasks, and transportation engineering challenges). Pre- and post-assessments will use validated spatial ability scales (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) and surveys on STEM identity. We will employ hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) to analyze growth trajectories and logistic regression to compare the efficacy of pre-test scores versus post-enrichment engagement metrics in predicting sustained STEM interest.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for a shift in educational resource allocation from screening to universal enrichment. It will contribute a validated framework for 'opportunity-centered' talent development, specifically linking scalable outreach activities to measurable gains in spatial reasoning, a key STEM aptitude.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies spatial ability, this study inverts the model to test if creating ability through enrichment makes initial identification less critical. It uniquely integrates Lakin's prior work on spatial assessment scales and STEM festival outreach into a cohesive intervention study.

proposals.title: Beyond Representation: A Longitudinal, Multi-Scale Analysis of STEM Identity and Life Outcomes for Marginalized Students
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 STEM education research strongly advocates for increasing representation and identification of marginalized students in STEM fields. However, it remains an open question whether these proximal gains in self-perception and engagement, often measured in outreach or classroom settings, translate into tangible long-term educational and career outcomes. This gap is critical for evaluating the true efficacy of equity-focused interventions and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while improved STEM identification and spatial ability are positively correlated with immediate engagement metrics, they are not sufficient predictors of long-term persistence and career attainment for marginalized students without concurrent, structural support systems.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-year, mixed-methods longitudinal study following cohorts from middle school through early post-secondary education. Using validated spatial ability scales (e.g., PSVT:R) and STEM identity/self-efficacy surveys, we will collect baseline and annual data. This will be linked to administrative records on course selection, performance, and college major declaration. We will employ structural equation modeling (SEM) to test causal pathways and latent growth curve modeling to analyze trajectories. Qualitative interviews will contextualize quantitative findings regarding structural barriers and supports.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, longitudinal evidence base for the relationship between representation, ability, and life outcomes, moving beyond correlational snapshots. It will offer the field a validated multi-scale assessment framework (individual, institutional) to evaluate long-term intervention impacts and identify critical leverage points for systemic support beyond initial engagement.
proposals.novelty: This study directly challenges the assumed linearity from 'identification' to 'outcome,' a core but untested tenet of many diversity initiatives. It uniquely integrates Lakin's expertise in spatial ability assessment with longitudinal life-course methodology to dissect this complex relationship.

proposals.title: Deconstructing Culture-Fairness: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of Contextual Influences on Spatial and Non-Cognitive Assessment Performance
proposals.motivation: Spatial ability and non-cognitive attributes (e.g., grit, mindset) are critical predictors for STEM success, yet their assessment often assumes culture-fairness. Pre-2026 research indicates that cultural context, including familiarity with test content, language, and problem-solving norms, can significantly bias performance, potentially misidentifying talent and reinforcing inequities in STEM education and research. This problem is acute in globalized research and outreach efforts, like those in Lakin's work on STEM festivals and K12 collaborations.
proposals.hypothesis: Performance on established spatial ability and non-cognitive measures will show systematic, interpretable variation across cultural and sub-cultural groups that is not attributable to underlying trait differences alone, but to contextual factors embedded in the assessment instruments and administration.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. First, we will administer a battery of established spatial tests (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) and non-cognitive scales (e.g., grit, engineering identity) to a diverse sample of middle/high school students from distinct cultural/regional backgrounds within the US. Quantitative analysis will use Differential Item Functioning (DIF) via logistic regression and Item Response Theory (IRT) models to flag biased items. Concurrently, we will conduct cognitive labs and semi-structured interviews to probe students' reasoning, interpretation of items, and contextual associations, using thematic analysis to identify cultural and experiential influences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence of cultural bias in common STEM aptitude measures, moving beyond assumption to documentation. It will yield a framework for evaluating and adapting assessments for specific cultural contexts, directly informing more equitable research practices and outreach activities in STEM education.
proposals.novelty: While DIF analysis is known, its application to spatial ability measures combined with qualitative cognitive labs to *explain* the sources of DIF represents a novel integration. This moves from detecting bias to diagnosing its cultural and contextual roots, a crucial step for creating genuinely fairer assessments.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Predictive Validity of Non-Cognitive Attributes for STEM Academic and Career Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in STEM education emphasizes cognitive skills like spatial ability, but the predictive power of non-cognitive factors (e.g., conscientiousness, leadership) for long-term outcomes remains underexplored. This gap is critical for designing effective interventions and advising students, as these attributes may be more malleable and equally indicative of success. Grounding this in Dr. Lakin's work on spatial scales and outreach provides a unique context to bridge cognitive and non-cognitive assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific non-cognitive attributes, measured during middle and high school, will significantly predict longitudinal outcomes in STEM academic performance and early career engagement, even after controlling for baseline cognitive abilities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal panel study, recruiting participants from existing K-12 STEM outreach cohorts (e.g., Transportation Festivals). Baseline data will include established cognitive measures (e.g., spatial ability tests) and non-cognitive scales (e.g., Big Five Inventory for conscientiousness, leadership self-efficacy). We will track participants for 5-8 years, collecting academic transcripts and career surveys. Predictive validity will be tested using hierarchical linear modeling and machine learning algorithms (specifically, regularized regression like LASSO and Random Forests) to identify which baseline attributes best predict long-term outcomes while controlling for demographic variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on which non-cognitive attributes are most predictive of STEM persistence, informing the design of targeted educational and outreach programs. It will extend Dr. Lakin's research on assessment scales by validating their long-term utility and integrating cognitive and non-cognitive frameworks for a more holistic understanding of student potential.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in longitudinally linking pre-college non-cognitive assessments from authentic outreach settings to concrete academic and career milestones in STEM, a connection rarely made in pre-2026 literature. Furthermore, applying predictive ML algorithms to this educational longitudinal data represents a methodological advance for the field.

proposals.title: Portfolio-Based Assessment for Spatial Talent Identification in STEM: A Framework for Middle and High School Students
proposals.motivation: Traditional standardized tests for identifying STEM talent often fail to capture complex, creative spatial reasoning and problem-solving abilities, potentially overlooking diverse student potential. Portfolio assessment, a well-established method in arts education, offers a holistic, process-oriented alternative but remains underexplored for spatial ability assessment in STEM contexts. This research addresses the need for more equitable and comprehensive talent identification methods that align with authentic engineering and design practices.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured portfolio review protocol, adapted from arts education, can reliably identify and characterize levels of spatial reasoning talent in middle and high school students, revealing dimensions of ability not captured by conventional psychometric scales. This method will demonstrate higher predictive validity for student engagement and performance in project-based STEM activities.
proposals.method: We will develop a digital portfolio framework where students document their process and final products from spatially demanding activities (e.g., engineering design challenges, 3D modeling tasks). Using a mixed-methods approach, we will first employ qualitative content analysis and thematic coding to identify key spatial ability attributes evident in portfolios. We will then train and validate a supervised machine learning model (e.g., a Random Forest or Support Vector Machine classifier) using features extracted from portfolio artifacts (e.g., complexity of sketches, iterative design steps, use of spatial transformations) to predict performance on established spatial ability scales (e.g., PSVT:R, MCT) and expert educator ratings of talent.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, validated assessment framework that operationalizes portfolio review for spatial talent identification, contributing to the literature on alternative assessment in STEM education. It will provide educators and researchers with a practical tool and rubric for recognizing and nurturing spatial creativity, thereby supporting more inclusive and engaging K-12 STEM outreach and talent development pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While portfolio assessment is common in the arts, its systematic application to identify and measure spatial reasoning talent within STEM education is novel. The integration of qualitative expert review with quantitative feature extraction and machine learning classification to create a scalable assessment model represents a significant methodological advance beyond existing, general spatial ability scales.

proposals.title: Dynamic Assessment of Persistence and Problem-Solving in STEM Students Through Telemetry in a Spatial Reasoning Game
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in STEM education highlights the critical role of non-cognitive traits like persistence and problem-solving for student success, yet these are often measured via static, self-report surveys. Educational games provide rich, authentic problem-solving contexts, but their telemetry is typically used for assessing cognitive skills or engagement, not for modeling dynamic non-cognitive states. This limits educators' ability to provide timely, targeted support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequences of in-game actions (e.g., time on task, error recovery patterns, tool use, and help-seeking behaviors) captured via telemetry can be used to construct valid, real-time computational models of student persistence and problem-solving strategy.
proposals.method: We will develop a game-based activity focused on spatial reasoning tasks (e.g., mental rotation, navigation puzzles) for middle/high school students. Telemetry data (clickstreams, timestamps, object interactions) will be logged. We will employ a multi-step analytical pipeline: 1) Feature engineering to extract metrics like pause frequency before failures and sequence similarity to expert solutions. 2) Application of Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) to identify latent problem-solving phases. 3) Validation of the model outputs against established survey instruments (e.g., Grit-S scale) and expert-coded video observations of problem-solving behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, validated framework for the dynamic, unobtrusive assessment of non-cognitive traits within authentic learning tasks. It will extend the researcher's prior work on spatial ability assessment by integrating a temporal, process-oriented dimension. The resulting analytics dashboard will provide a prototype tool for educators to identify students needing motivational or strategic support during STEM outreach activities.
proposals.novelty: While game analytics are common, their application to model the *dynamic evolution* of persistence and problem-solving strategy in real-time, specifically within spatial STEM tasks for K-12 students, represents a novel synthesis of educational data mining and non-cognitive assessment.

proposals.title: Developing and Validating Ultra-Brief Classroom Screening Batteries for Spatial Ability and STEM-Relevant Non-Cognitive Attributes
proposals.motivation: Spatial ability and non-cognitive attributes like mindset and grit are strong predictors of STEM success, but existing validated measures are too long for practical classroom use by teachers. This creates a barrier to early identification of students who could benefit from targeted spatial or motivational interventions. There is a critical need for psychometrically sound, ultra-short instruments that can be administered in under 10 minutes without specialized training.
proposals.hypothesis: Using modern psychometric methods, it is possible to create ultra-brief (≤5 item) screening batteries for spatial visualization and STEM-relevant non-cognitive attributes that maintain acceptable reliability (α ≥ .70) and strong criterion validity with their full-length counterparts.
proposals.method: We will employ a three-phase design. First, we will curate item pools from established instruments (e.g., PSVT:R for spatial ability, GRIT-S for perseverance). Second, we will use a combination of classical test theory and item response theory (specifically, Rasch modeling and 2PL IRT) to identify the most discriminative and informative items. Third, we will validate the short forms against the full measures and external STEM engagement criteria using confirmatory factor analysis and regression models in a diverse sample of middle and high school students.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide educators with a practical, evidence-based toolkit for rapid student assessment, directly supporting data-informed instructional decisions and personalized STEM outreach. It will advance the methodology of scale development for constrained educational contexts and bridge the gap between research on individual differences and classroom practice.
proposals.novelty: While short forms exist, this project uniquely focuses on creating a *cohesive battery* of cross-domain measures (spatial and non-cognitive) optimized specifically for the severe time constraints of a K-12 classroom, moving beyond simple item reduction to multi-method psychometric optimization for applied use.

proposals.title: Integrating Novel Spatial and Engineering Reasoning Measures into the High School Longitudinal Study (HSLS): A Framework for Scalable Assessment and Predictive Modeling
proposals.motivation: Large-scale national datasets like the High School Longitudinal Study (HSLS) primarily track traditional academic outcomes and demographics, lacking granular measures of cognitive abilities like spatial reasoning and non-cognitive attributes crucial for STEM persistence. This gap limits researchers' and policymakers' ability to identify early, malleable predictors of STEM pathway success, particularly for underrepresented groups. Integrating validated, novel measures into these established longitudinal frameworks is essential for understanding the developmental trajectories of 21st-century STEM competencies.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating validated, computer-adaptive measures of spatial ability and engineering design reasoning into the HSLS framework will significantly improve the prediction of student STEM major selection and persistence in postsecondary education, beyond the variance explained by traditional math scores and demographics.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate a supplemental digital assessment module for integration into future HSLS waves. For spatial ability, we will implement a computer-adaptive test (CAT) using item response theory (IRT) and a multidimensional Rasch model, drawing from established instruments like the Purdue Spatial Visualization Tests. For engineering reasoning, we will design scenario-based tasks using an Evidence-Centered Design (ECD) framework, with automated scoring logic to evaluate attributes like systems thinking and iterative design. These modules will be piloted for feasibility and psychometric quality before proposed national deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological blueprint and validated instrument suite for embedding rich cognitive and non-cognitive constructs into national surveys. It will create the first longitudinal, nationally representative dataset explicitly linking nuanced spatial and engineering reasoning profiles to long-term STEM educational outcomes, enabling powerful new analyses on skill development and equity.
proposals.novelty: While spatial ability research exists, its integration into a major federal longitudinal study using adaptive, modern psychometrics is unprecedented. The combined focus on scaling engineering-specific reasoning attributes—beyond general science interest—within a national database represents a significant advance over existing, smaller-scale studies.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Instructional Gaps: Using Student Spatial Ability Screening to Target In-Service Teacher Professional Development
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research consistently shows spatial ability is a strong predictor of STEM success and is malleable through training, yet it is often neglected in K-12 curricula. Teachers frequently lack the pedagogical content knowledge to identify and nurture diverse spatial talents, particularly in underrepresented student groups. This gap limits equitable access to STEM pathways and fails to leverage a key cognitive skill for future problem-solving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patterns in student performance on a comprehensive spatial ability assessment can be used to identify specific, actionable gaps in teachers' instructional capacity. Furthermore, targeted professional development informed by these patterns will lead to measurable improvements in teachers' ability to foster spatial reasoning across a diverse student population.
proposals.method: We will administer a validated, multi-scale spatial ability assessment (e.g., Mental Rotation, Spatial Visualization, Cross-Sections) to middle school students in STEM classrooms. Using pre-2026 machine learning techniques, specifically a Random Forest classifier and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values, we will analyze the assessment data to identify which spatial sub-skills show the weakest performance and greatest demographic disparity per classroom. This diagnostic output will be mapped to a framework of teacher competencies (e.g., ability to design mental rotation activities, explain 2D-3D transformations) to generate individualized professional development profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes a novel, data-driven methodology for linking student learning diagnostics directly to teacher professional development needs, moving beyond generic training. It will produce a scalable framework for identifying instructional gaps in spatial pedagogy, thereby supporting more equitable and effective STEM education. The resulting tools and protocols will be directly applicable to engineer-educator collaborations in outreach settings.
proposals.novelty: While student assessment is common, its direct, algorithmic use to diagnose *teacher* instructional gaps in spatial ability nurturing is a novel inversion of the typical problem. The approach integrates psychometric scales with explainable AI (XAI) to provide interpretable feedback for educator development, a non-obvious application of pre-2026 ML methods in educational research.

proposals.title: From Moderators to Architects: A Framework for Educator-Defined Adaptation of Spatial Reasoning Interventions in STEM Outreach
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on STEM outreach and spatial ability interventions often treats educators as passive implementers of researcher-designed programs. This overlooks the critical, contextual expertise teachers possess regarding their students' needs and classroom dynamics. A shift towards educator-defined implementation is crucial for creating sustainable, scalable, and deeply impactful learning experiences that move beyond one-off outreach events.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured framework enabling educators to architect, rather than merely moderate, spatial reasoning activities will lead to interventions with higher perceived relevance, greater integration into existing curricula, and improved student engagement compared to a standardized program.
proposals.method: We will develop and test an 'Educator Architecture Framework' (EAF) through a design-based research partnership with K-12 STEM educators. The EAF will be a co-designed toolkit of modular spatial reasoning activities (e.g., mental rotation, cross-sectioning) and assessment items, informed by pre-2026 validated spatial ability scales. Educator expertise will define the selection, sequencing, and contextualization of these modules. We will employ mixed-methods analysis, comparing pre/post spatial ability assessments (using established instruments like the PSVT:R) and qualitative analysis of educator interviews and classroom observations across EAF and control (standard program) conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practitioner-centered model for designing STEM outreach and spatial training, shifting the paradigm from program fidelity to educator agency. It will provide empirical evidence on how educator judgment influences program efficacy and student outcomes, offering a scalable template for researcher-educator collaboration that prioritizes sustainable integration over one-time delivery.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines educator moderation of programs, this study inverts the relationship, positioning the educator as the primary architect of the intervention's core design. It uniquely applies principles of participatory design and teacher agency specifically to the domain of spatial ability development, an area typically dominated by standardized, researcher-controlled testing and training paradigms.

proposals.title: Lateral Transfer of STEM Outreach Programs: A Framework for Scaling Across Dissimilar Educational Contexts
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on scaling educational interventions predominantly focuses on replication in demographically or structurally similar settings, limiting generalizability. This study addresses a critical gap by investigating whether successful, research-backed STEM outreach programs (e.g., Transportation Festivals) can be effectively adapted to radically different contexts, such as from well-resourced suburban schools to rural or under-resourced urban districts. Understanding the mechanisms of lateral transfer is essential for broadening equitable access to high-quality STEM experiences and for developing robust, context-agnostic scaling theories.
proposals.hypothesis: The core program logic model and key engagement principles of a validated STEM outreach intervention can be successfully transferred to dissimilar educational contexts through a structured adaptation framework that modifies surface-level activities while preserving underlying pedagogical and motivational constructs.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, quasi-experimental design. First, we will deconstruct a successful 'source' program (e.g., a Transportation Festival) using a logic model analysis to identify its core components (e.g., spatial reasoning tasks, industry mentor interactions). We will then partner with schools in a dissimilar context (e.g., rural schools with limited industry access) to co-design an adapted version. Student outcomes, including spatial ability (assessed via validated instruments like the PSVT:R) and STEM identity, will be measured pre- and post-intervention. Comparative analysis will use ANCOVA to control for prior ability, and qualitative data from educator logs and student focus groups will inform the adaptation process.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical framework for the lateral transfer of educational programs, moving beyond simple replication. It will generate empirical evidence on which program attributes are context-sensitive versus robust, directly informing policy and practice for equitable STEM outreach scaling. The study will also advance methodological approaches in implementation science for education by testing transfer across a high-contrast design.
proposals.novelty: This proposal explicitly challenges the conventional 'scale-to-similar' paradigm in educational intervention research by intentionally targeting dissimilar contexts for transfer. It integrates Lakin's prior work on spatial ability assessment and industry-educator collaborations into a novel investigation of scaling theory, rather than just scaling practice.

proposals.title: Unmasking Developmental Trajectories: A Longitudinal Analysis of Latent Sex Differences in Spatial Ability Using Multi-Dimensional Item Response Theory
proposals.motivation: Research on sex differences in spatial ability, a key predictor for STEM interest and success, has produced mixed findings, often relying on cross-sectional data and manifest (observed) test scores. Pre-2026 literature suggests that manifest scores may mask underlying latent differences due to measurement invariance issues, differential item functioning, or developmental pacing. Understanding the true nature of these differences across K-12 and undergraduate development is critical for designing equitable educational interventions and outreach activities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longitudinal analysis using latent variable modeling will reveal significant sex-based differences in the developmental trajectories of specific spatial reasoning sub-constructs (e.g., mental rotation, spatial visualization) that are obscured in analyses of manifest composite scores.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal cohort-sequential design, collecting spatial ability data from middle school through undergraduate students over three years. Using pre-2026 psychometric techniques, we will apply Multi-Dimensional Item Response Theory (MIRT) models to estimate latent ability scores on distinct spatial subscales at each time point. We will then use Latent Growth Curve Modeling (LGCM) and Growth Mixture Modeling (GMM) to compare developmental trajectories between sexes. Model fit will be assessed using comparative fit indices (CFI, TLI) and information criteria (AIC, BIC).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodological framework for analyzing developmental changes in cognitive abilities, moving beyond simple mean comparisons. It will provide empirical evidence on whether and how sex differences in spatial reasoning emerge and evolve, directly informing the design of targeted, age-appropriate STEM outreach and curriculum activities highlighted in Dr. Lakin's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While cross-sectional studies on sex differences are common, this proposal integrates longitudinal design with advanced latent variable modeling (MIRT and GMM) specifically to test the 'masking hypothesis,' a non-obvious claim that requires tracking the same individuals over time to disentangle measurement from true developmental change.

proposals.title: Cross-Cultural and Developmental Validation of Latent Spatial Ability Constructs: A Multi-Sample Confirmatory Factor Analysis
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Lakin's work on assessing spatial ability through higher-order LLM, has identified latent difference patterns in spatial reasoning. However, the generalizability of these constructs across diverse populations remains unverified. This is critical for developing equitable STEM education and outreach, as spatial ability is a known predictor of success in STEM fields, yet its measurement may be biased by cultural or developmental factors.
proposals.hypothesis: The latent structure of spatial ability, as measured by established and novel digital assessments, will demonstrate measurement invariance across age groups (K-12 to adult) and cultural/educational backgrounds, confirming its validity as a universal cognitive construct.
proposals.method: We will replicate the core assessment methodology from prior work, using a battery of validated spatial tasks (e.g., mental rotation, spatial visualization) and novel LLM-driven interactive activities. Data will be collected from stratified samples across three age bands (middle school, high school, undergraduate) and at least two distinct cultural/educational contexts (e.g., U.S. public schools and an international partner). We will employ multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MG-CFA) using structural equation modeling software (e.g., Mplus) to test for configural, metric, and scalar invariance of the latent spatial ability model.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust evidence for the universality or cultural specificity of spatial ability constructs, directly informing the design of inclusive STEM curricula and K-12 outreach activities. It will establish a validated, cross-culturally fair assessment framework for future research on spatial reasoning development and intervention efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While spatial ability is widely studied, systematic tests of measurement invariance for modern, digitally-administered assessments across combined age and cultural spectra are rare. This work uniquely integrates developmental and cross-cultural psychology methods with STEM education research to validate foundational cognitive constructs.

proposals.title: Investigating Spatial Reasoning Assessment Equity: A Differential Item Functioning Analysis of the Cognitive Abilities Test (CogAT)
proposals.motivation: Research on spatial ability, critical for STEM success, often reports significant variance differences across demographic groups (e.g., gender, socioeconomic status) in aggregate scores. However, pre-2026 work rarely isolates whether these differences stem from a true construct-level disparity or from specific test items that function differently for different groups. This ambiguity hinders the development of equitable assessments and targeted educational interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of CogAT spatial reasoning items exhibits significant Differential Item Functioning (DIF), and that these items disproportionately contribute to observed group variance in total scores, potentially masking the true underlying distribution of spatial reasoning ability.
proposals.method: We will analyze anonymized, item-level response data from a large, diverse sample of K-12 students who have taken the CogAT's spatial batteries. DIF analysis will be conducted using established pre-2026 logistic regression and Mantel-Haenszel procedures, implemented in R packages like 'difR'. We will control for overall ability using total score matching and examine DIF across multiple demographic attributes (e.g., gender, ELL status) to identify items with uniform and non-uniform DIF. The contribution of flagged items to total score variance will be quantified.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a granular, evidence-based audit of a widely used spatial ability assessment, identifying specific items that may introduce measurement bias. The findings will contribute directly to the refinement of more equitable spatial reasoning instruments and inform the design of K-12 outreach and research activities by highlighting which specific spatial tasks require focused instructional support.
proposals.novelty: While DIF is a standard psychometric technique, its application to dissect group differences in spatial ability assessments, particularly the CogAT, is limited. This study uniquely bridges rigorous psychometric analysis with the practical goal of improving equity in spatial reasoning research and STEM education diagnostics.

proposals.title: Mapping the Cognitive Web: A Network Psychometric Analysis of Item-Level Interactions in the Cognitive Abilities Test (CogAT)
proposals.motivation: Traditional latent factor models used to analyze cognitive assessments like the CogAT assume that observed correlations between items are solely due to shared, underlying abilities (e.g., verbal, quantitative, spatial). This may oversimplify the complex, interactive nature of cognitive processes. Pre-2026, network psychometrics has emerged as a powerful alternative in clinical psychology, modeling symptoms as directly interacting systems, but its application to high-stakes educational ability testing remains limited.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a psychometric network model of CogAT item responses will reveal significant direct interactions between specific items across traditional subtest boundaries, suggesting a web of cognitive processes not fully captured by standard latent factor scoring.
proposals.method: We will apply Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) and Ising model estimation to anonymized, item-level CogAT response data from a large, diverse sample of K-12 students. Using the `psychonetrics` and `qgraph` packages in R, we will estimate regularized partial correlation networks to identify unique conditional dependencies between items after controlling for all others. Model stability will be assessed via bootstrapping, and the resulting network structure will be compared to the traditional three-factor CogAT model using confirmatory factor analysis fit indices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, fine-grained map of cognitive skill interactions as measured by a widely used instrument, contributing to fundamental theories of cognitive structure. Practically, it could inform more nuanced diagnostic profiling for gifted and talented programs and suggest new, network-informed approaches to developing spatial and reasoning activities for STEM outreach.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to personality and psychopathology data, its rigorous application to a major, multi-scale educational ability battery like the CogAT to challenge latent variable orthodoxy is novel. It bridges advanced psychometric theory with the practical analysis of a dataset central to research on student abilities.

proposals.title: Testing Measurement Invariance of the Cognitive Abilities Test (CogAT) Across Sex Using Multi-Group Item Response Theory
proposals.motivation: The Cognitive Abilities Test (CogAT) is widely used in K-12 education for identifying gifted students and informing instructional practices. Persistent concerns exist regarding potential measurement bias, particularly across sexes, which could lead to inequitable identification and educational placement if the test measures latent abilities differently for male and female students. Rigorous, model-based testing for measurement invariance is essential to ensure the fairness and validity of this high-stakes assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CogAT will demonstrate partial measurement invariance, where the majority of items function equivalently across sexes, but specific items, potentially in spatial reasoning or verbal domains, will exhibit differential item functioning (DIF).
proposals.method: We will apply multi-group confirmatory factor analysis (MG-CFA) and Item Response Theory (IRT) models to existing, large-scale CogAT score data. Using the `mirt` package in R, we will implement a sequential likelihood ratio test approach, first testing for configural invariance, then metric (loading) invariance, and finally scalar (intercept) invariance. For items flagged for DIF, we will conduct specific model comparisons (e.g., comparing a 2PL IRT model to a model with group-specific parameters) to quantify the nature and magnitude of the bias.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous, psychometric evaluation of sex-based measurement invariance on the CogAT, moving beyond simple mean-score comparisons. The findings will offer concrete evidence to test developers and educational practitioners about the fairness of the instrument and identify specific item content that may require revision. It will also serve as a methodological exemplar for applying advanced IRT techniques to evaluate bias in widely-used educational assessments.
proposals.novelty: While DIF analysis is established, its rigorous application to the full CogAT battery using modern, accessible IRT software to test for sex invariance is not comprehensively documented in pre-2026 literature. This work directly bridges Lakin's expertise in spatial ability assessment and educational outreach with advanced quantitative methods, applying research-scale techniques to a pervasive tool in K-12 practice.

proposals.title: Unmasking Latent Sex Differences in Fluid Intelligence: The Moderating Role of High-Fidelity Spatial Abilities
proposals.motivation: Research on sex differences in cognitive abilities, particularly in fluid intelligence (Gf) and spatial reasoning, often reports small or inconsistent mean-level differences at the population level. However, these aggregate findings may obscure a more complex individual-differences phenomenon where exceptionally strong performance on specific, highly relevant abilities (e.g., mental rotation) could mask underlying sex-based variance in broader Gf. Understanding this potential masking effect is critical for refining cognitive assessment models and for designing equitable, targeted educational interventions in STEM fields, where spatial ability is a known predictor of success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high performance on specific, narrow spatial abilities (e.g., mental rotation, spatial visualization) will statistically suppress the observable sex-based variance in broader fluid intelligence (Gf) tasks for individuals, such that sex differences in latent Gf are larger when controlling for these specific abilities.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, diverse sample of university STEM students. Participants will complete a battery of established cognitive tests: Raven's Progressive Matrices and a complex figure series task for Gf, and the Mental Rotations Test and Paper Folding Test for specific spatial abilities. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will construct a latent Gf factor and specific spatial ability factors. We will then employ moderated nonlinear factor analysis (MNLFA) to test for differential item functioning (DIF) and factor mean differences by sex, with specific spatial ability scores as moderators. This will quantify how spatial ability levels alter the measurement and manifestation of sex differences in the latent Gf construct.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, person-centered perspective on the long-debated topic of cognitive sex differences, moving beyond mean comparisons. It will contribute a methodological framework for identifying moderator variables that mask group differences in latent traits. The findings will directly inform the development of more nuanced cognitive assessment practices and have implications for theories of intelligence structure and the identification of potential in STEM education.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined sex differences in spatial and Gf tasks separately, this proposal innovatively tests the *interaction* between them, positing specific abilities as a suppressor variable for group differences in a broader latent trait, a hypothesis not explicitly tested in pre-2026 literature.

This provides a robust evaluation of whether the training's intended outcomes persist and are functionally applied, not just whether participants remain formally enrolled in a program.

ideas.title: Scaffolding the Engineering Epistemic Frame: Making Disciplinary Practice Observable and Coachable
ideas.description: This research investigates how learners can be guided to adopt the core epistemic frame of engineering—its characteristic ways of investigating, problem-solving, and justifying decisions—without requiring a prior conscious identity as an engineer. The study designs and tests a pedagogical system that uses authentic, constrained design tasks coupled with real-time feedback protocols focused on observable practices like trade-off analysis, iterative testing, and evidence-based argumentation. By making the components of the engineering frame explicit and coachable, the approach aims to robustly develop engineering competence, which may then organically support identity formation as a downstream outcome.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Measure Values-Identity Alignment in Engineering Careers
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Ecological Niche Modeling, this research proposes to develop and validate a novel survey methodology to quantify the alignment between personal career values and professional identity perceptions in engineering. In ecology, a species' fit within a habitat is measured by modeling its niche within a multidimensional environmental space. Adapted here, an individual's career values (autonomy, creativity, stability, impact, etc.) form their personal 'values niche,' while their perceptions of the engineering field's characteristics form the 'professional environment.' The research will create survey scales to map both constructs into a common multidimensional space. Using techniques analogous to niche overlap indices (e.g., Schoener's D or Hellinger distance), the study will produce a validated, continuous 'values-environment alignment' score. This metric will allow researchers to precisely measure the degree of congruence or mismatch, enabling stronger predictive models of career satisfaction, retention, and identity development within engineering.

ideas.title: Critical Periods and Foundational Imprints: Re-evaluating the Stability of First-Year Perceptions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that early perceptions (e.g., of university, a new job) are highly malleable and shaped primarily by later experiences. Drawing an analogy from Developmental Neurobiology and its 'critical period' solution, we posit that first-year perceptions may instead create a stable, foundational imprint—akin to early neural architecture—that filters and shapes the interpretation of all subsequent pivotal experiences. The adapted research idea investigates this by longitudinally tracking cohorts to test if early-formed schemas demonstrate stability and act as a formative lens, thereby predicting long-term outcomes not despite, but through, their interaction with later events.

ideas.title: Evaluating how organizational onboarding designs shape professional identity in pharmacy and engineering
ideas.description: This research investigates how the design of initial, structured professional experiences—such as training interventions in large pharmacy chains and introductory course sequences in engineering schools—systematically influences the development of early professional identity. By applying identity development theories from education to workplace training, and vice-versa, it enables a direct comparison of pedagogical and organizational socialization strategies across two distinct, high-stakes fields. The study aims to identify universal design principles and field-specific nuances that most effectively foster a robust, ethical, and competent professional self-concept, providing evidence to improve both educational curricula and corporate onboarding programs.

The study will measure pharmacists' attitudes and intentions before and after exposure to the micro-training, then track their actual IIS usage data over 6-12 months. This allows for a causal analysis of whether the designed intervention is sufficient to bridge the 'intention-behavior gap' for IIS participation, moving beyond correlation to identify an effective, practical lever for improving data quality and completeness.

ideas.title: The Identity Resilience Threshold: When Strong Engineering Identity Triggers Disengagement
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the linear assumption that stronger engineering identity always increases persistence by proposing an analogical model from ecology: the Allee effect. In population biology, the Allee effect describes how population fitness increases with density to an optimum, after which negative density-dependent factors cause decline. Analogously, we hypothesize that engineering identity strength has a curvilinear relationship with persistence. Up to an optimal 'Identity Resilience Threshold,' a stronger identity provides motivation and belonging. However, beyond this threshold, the identity can become a source of pressure, perfectionism, and psychological brittleness. When faced with inevitable setbacks, conflicts with professional reality, or challenges to the self-concept, individuals with an overly strong and rigid identity may experience greater disillusionment and be more likely to disengage, contrary to the prevailing assumption. The adapted research idea involves identifying this threshold and investigating the specific mechanisms—such as fear of identity-inconsistent feedback, reduced adaptive flexibility, or internalized stereotype threat—that transform a beneficial identity into a liability for persistence in engineering.

ideas.title: Applying Conservation Biology's 'Flagship Species' Pedagogy to Altruistic Engineering Students
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Conservation Biology to address the pedagogical challenge of engaging altruistically-oriented engineering students. In environmental education, the 'flagship species' approach uses a charismatic animal to generate public support, which is then leveraged to teach about complex ecosystem preservation. Analogously, this research will design and test pedagogical interventions where students first apply engineering fundamentals to a narrowly scoped, highly relatable humanitarian 'flagship' project. The intervention's core is a structured reflection and scaffolding phase that explicitly connects the skills and knowledge from this concrete project to their authentic application in broader, systemic socio-technical problems. This aims to help students see their standard coursework not as abstract hurdles, but as authentic and necessary practice for achieving their altruistic goals on a larger scale.

ideas.title: Adapting the Theory of Planned Behavior's 'Intention-Behavior Gap' Framework to Predict Sustained IIS Participation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-studied 'intention-behavior gap' from environmental psychology, where positive attitudes towards recycling often fail to predict actual, sustained recycling behavior. The analogous solution involved identifying and measuring moderating variables—like perceived behavioral control, habit formation, and removing contextual barriers—that bridge intention and long-term action. Adapted to the IIS context, the research would move beyond testing a simple correlation between pharmacist intention and participation. Instead, it would employ a longitudinal design to model how factors such as system usability, integration into dispensing workflow, automated prompts, organizational support, and the development of habitual use moderate the relationship between initial positive intention and consistent, long-term IIS data entry, thereby challenging the assumption that intention is sufficient for sustained participation.

ideas.description: This study investigates whether expanding IIS training to include pharmacy technicians and front-end staff, as opposed to pharmacists only, creates a more durable intervention that reduces or eliminates the need for ongoing booster sessions. It will compare the long-term data completeness and accuracy from pharmacies that receive the expanded team training against those with traditional pharmacist-only training, measuring decay rates over 12-18 months. The core hypothesis is that by integrating IIS tasks into the workflows of multiple staff roles, the pharmacy develops internal reinforcement mechanisms and redundancy, making the system less vulnerable to staff turnover and knowledge attrition. This tests if a strategic, one-time investment in team-based training can yield a more sustainable and cost-effective model for high-quality IIS participation than repeated support for a single key individual.

ideas.title: Altruistic definitions are inherently problematic
ideas.description: Invert the finding: perhaps altruistic definitions are adaptive and signal a broader, more sustainable view of engineering.
ideas.sourceTitle: Am I an engineer yet? Perceptions of engineering and identity among first year students

ideas.title: Apply nudge theory to increase student completion
ideas.description: Use behavioral economics nudges to reduce procrastination and increase project completion rates among students.
ideas.sourceTitle: Getting Everyone to the Fair: Supporting Teachers in Broadening Participation in Science and Engineering Fairs

ideas.title: Co-design and evaluate scalable, context-adaptive IIS training for diverse pharmacy ecosystems
ideas.description: This research employs a sequential, iterative mixed-methods approach. First, participatory design workshops are conducted separately with pharmacists from large chain pharmacies and integrated health systems to co-create initial, context-specific IIS training prototypes. These distinct prototypes are then implemented and evaluated for effectiveness within their respective settings using a pragmatic trial design. The qualitative and quantitative findings from this real-world testing are fed back into a second round of participatory design, creating a closed-loop system that continuously adapts the training to organizational workflows and barriers, ultimately producing a validated, scalable training framework that is inherently adaptable to different pharmacy ecosystems.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map IIS Training Program Adoption Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from conservation biology, where Species Distribution Models (SDMs) are used to predict a species' potential range based on environmental variables. The analogous solution involves modeling the relationship between a species' presence and key habitat factors to guide conservation efforts. Adapted to the IIS training context, we propose using implementation science variables (e.g., organizational readiness, compatibility, relative advantage) as the 'environmental' predictors. By modeling these factors from successful early implementation sites, we can create a predictive 'adoption landscape' map. This model would then be used to strategically identify and prioritize future rollout sites with the highest predicted suitability for adoption, thereby increasing the efficiency and success of the scale-up process, much like targeting conservation resources to the most viable habitats.

ideas.title: Investigate faculty perspectives on normative expectations
ideas.description: Interview faculty at both community colleges and four-year universities to understand their rationales for setting specific academic norms.
ideas.sourceTitle: Unparallel Pathways: Exploring How Divergent Academic Norms Contribute to the Transfer Shock of STEM Students

ideas.title: Teacher buy-in is sufficient for success
ideas.description: Invert the assumption that positive teacher expectations guarantee successful, equitable student recruitment and project completion.
ideas.sourceTitle: Getting Everyone to the Fair: Supporting Teachers in Broadening Participation in Science and Engineering Fairs

ideas.title: Scale up to national or international assessment data
ideas.description: Apply the bifactor modeling approach to large-scale datasets like PISA or NAEP.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sex Differences in Fluid Reasoning: Manifest and Latent Estimates from the Cognitive Abilities Test

ideas.title: Create a train-the-trainer rural facilitator model
ideas.description: Scale by training local facilitators to sustain and spread the program independently.
ideas.sourceTitle: Nurturing STEM talent in rural setting

ideas.title: Use social network analysis for implementation mapping
ideas.description: Map communication and influence networks to understand how program practices spread among educators.
ideas.sourceTitle: Fidelity of Implementation: Understanding Why and When Programs Work

ideas.title: Resource support alone solves equity issues
ideas.description: Challenge that providing materials and training is sufficient to overcome systemic barriers in under-resourced schools.
ideas.sourceTitle: Getting Everyone to the Fair: Supporting Teachers in Broadening Participation in Science and Engineering Fairs

ideas.title: Identity formation is always beneficial for persistence
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that stronger engineering identity always increases persistence; it might create pressure or disillusionment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Am I an engineer yet? Perceptions of engineering and identity among first year students

ideas.title: Dual problem: institutional transfer readiness
ideas.description: Measure how well receiving institutions are prepared to support transfers, not just student preparedness.
ideas.sourceTitle: STEMing the Shock: Examining Transfer Shock and Its Impact on STEM Major and Enrollment Persistence

ideas.title: The Double-Edged Sword of System Identity: When Enrollment and Identity Formation Undermine Data Integrity and Persistence
ideas.description: This research challenges the dual assumptions that enrollment in a system equates to high-quality use and that forming a strong identity with that system is universally beneficial. It proposes to study immunization information systems (IIS) or similar health data infrastructures to examine if and when users (e.g., providers, clinic staff) who develop a strong 'system-identity'—internalizing the system's importance as part of their professional role—might subsequently exhibit lower data entry quality or even drop out of using the system. The core hypothesis is that a strong system-identity could create performance pressure, foster disillusionment when system flaws are encountered, or lead to complacency, thereby decoupling enrollment from active, high-quality use and making identity a risk factor for non-persistence rather than a protective asset.

ideas.title: Enrollment as a 'Dark Matter' Problem: Measuring the Gap Between System Registration and Data Completeness
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that enrollment in an Immunization Information System (IIS) equates to active, high-quality use. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where 'dark matter' is detected by the gap between observable matter and total gravitational mass, this research reconceptualizes enrollment as merely the 'visible matter.' The analogous solution—quantifying the unseen by measuring discrepancies in observable effects—is adapted to develop metrics for a 'data completeness gap.' The study would measure the discrepancy between a healthcare provider's formal enrollment status and the actual completeness, timeliness, and accuracy of their vaccination data entries in the IIS. By doing so, it moves the field from a binary understanding of participation to a continuous spectrum, identifying the true scale of the 'dark data' problem and enabling targeted interventions to improve effective IIS utilization.

ideas.title: Use implementation science frameworks for rollout
ideas.description: Apply frameworks like RE-AIM to plan and evaluate the scale-up of IIS training programs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Increasing use of immunization information systems for routine vaccinations in independent community pharmacies: A randomized controlled trial

ideas.title: Sustained Professional Practice Requires Unconscious Competence, Not Just Conscious Intention
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that for a professional behavior like consistent IIS data entry to be sustained long-term, it must transition from being a conscious act driven by intention to an unconscious, integrated component of professional identity—akin to how an engineer might solve problems without consciously labeling the activity. It challenges the assumption in health services research that positive attitudes and intentions are sufficient predictors of long-term adherence. Instead, the study would measure whether pharmacists who have internalized data stewardship as a fundamental, non-negotiable aspect of being a pharmacist—rather than just a task they 'intend' to do—demonstrate more consistent and durable IIS participation, independent of fluctuating conscious motivation.

ideas.title: Invert causality: variability causes mean difference perceptions
ideas.description: Study if greater male variability creates statistical artifacts that are misinterpreted as mean differences.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sex Differences in Fluid Reasoning: Manifest and Latent Estimates from the Cognitive Abilities Test

ideas.title: Use machine learning for predictive bias detection
ideas.description: Apply ML fairness algorithms to detect and interpret group biases in cognitive test predictions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Sex Differences in Fluid Reasoning: Manifest and Latent Estimates from the Cognitive Abilities Test

ideas.title: Test training in chain pharmacies and health systems
ideas.description: Evaluate if the IIS training intervention is effective in large chain pharmacies and integrated health systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Increasing use of immunization information systems for routine vaccinations in independent community pharmacies: A randomized controlled trial

ideas.title: Apply ecological momentary assessment from psychology
ideas.description: Use frequent, real-time surveys to capture the dynamic psychosocial experience of transfer shock.
ideas.sourceTitle: STEMing the Shock: Examining Transfer Shock and Its Impact on STEM Major and Enrollment Persistence

ideas.title: Challenge that four-year norms are inherently superior
ideas.description: Investigate if community college academic norms could be beneficial or more equitable, and if four-year norms create unnecessary barriers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Unparallel Pathways: Exploring How Divergent Academic Norms Contribute to the Transfer Shock of STEM Students

ideas.title: Assessing Post-Pandemic Cognitive Load and Receptivity via Just-in-Time Micro-Training
ideas.description: This study develops a 5-minute, just-in-time micro-training module (e.g., on data security or a new protocol) embedded into a routine login workflow. It will be deployed and evaluated in a post-COVID-19 context to investigate whether the collective pandemic experience has created a lasting shift in cognitive bandwidth, training receptivity, and compliance behavior for brief, mandatory interventions. By comparing uptake, performance, and attitudinal data to pre-pandemic baselines for similar micro-tasks, the research can isolate the pandemic's legacy effects on workforce capacity for micro-learning. This approach transforms the micro-training into both an intervention and a precise measurement probe for post-crisis cognitive and behavioral baselines.

ideas.title: Use sequence analysis from sociology
ideas.description: Map typical academic event sequences after transfer to identify critical junctures for intervention.
ideas.sourceTitle: STEMing the Shock: Examining Transfer Shock and Its Impact on STEM Major and Enrollment Persistence

ideas.title: Relax assumption that programs have stable definitions
ideas.description: Treat program definitions as dynamic and co-constructed by implementers and students over time.
ideas.sourceTitle: Fidelity of Implementation: Understanding Why and When Programs Work

ideas.title: Applying the 'Just-in-Time' Paradigm to Overcome IIS Training Barriers
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that training (knowledge) is the primary barrier to Immunization Information System (IIS) use by drawing an analogy from manufacturing. In supply chain management, the 'Just-in-Time' (JIT) system solved analogous barriers of high upfront costs and inventory complexity by delivering components precisely when needed for production, not before. Adapting this solution, the research would investigate if a 'Just-in-Time Learning' paradigm can overcome IIS adoption barriers. It would test whether factors like time and workflow integration are addressed more effectively by providing clinicians with embedded, context-sensitive guidance (e.g., pop-up alerts with brief how-to videos, order-set-integrated prompts) at the point of care, compared to the traditional barrier of mandatory, lengthy upfront training sessions. The goal is to determine if reducing the cognitive and temporal cost of accessing knowledge in real-time is a more critical lever for adoption than ensuring comprehensive prior knowledge.

ideas.title: Test intervention in diverse worksite settings
ideas.description: Replicate the study in different industries and company sizes beyond a single hospital.
ideas.sourceTitle: Pharmacist-led motivational interviewing for diabetes medication adherence in a worksite wellness program.

ideas.title: Challenge that universal screening is the optimal identification strategy
ideas.description: Investigate if targeted, group-specific identification methods outperform universal screening for equity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finding the missing Einsteins: Expanding the breadth of cognitive and noncognitive measures used in academic services

ideas.title: Apply organizational culture analysis to academic norms
ideas.description: Use ethnographic methods from organizational studies to map the 'cultures' of teaching and learning in different institutional types.
ideas.sourceTitle: Unparallel Pathways: Exploring How Divergent Academic Norms Contribute to the Transfer Shock of STEM Students

ideas.title: Apply Participatory Action Research from Community Ecology to Co-Design IIS Training with Pharmacists
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of Participatory Action Research (PAR) from the field of Community Ecology and Environmental Management, where local communities are engaged as co-researchers to develop sustainable resource management practices. The analogous solution involves iterative cycles of collaborative planning, action, observation, and reflection with stakeholders treated as context experts. Adapted to the research context, this method would be used to co-create Immunization Information System (IIS) training with pharmacists by establishing them as continuous co-researchers. The process would involve iterative cycles where pharmacists participate in defining training needs, prototyping modules, implementing them in their workflow, and jointly evaluating outcomes, thereby creating a dynamic, practitioner-owned training program that is precisely tailored to the evolving challenges and opportunities within pharmacy-based immunization.

ideas.title: Comparative Effectiveness Trial of Scalable Training Models to Overcome the Primary IIS Implementation Barrier
ideas.description: This research implements a multi-arm, comparative effectiveness trial across a national network of states with diverse IIS platforms. It scales different training intervention models, each designed to target a hypothesized primary barrier (e.g., a time-efficient model, a model with integrated billing support, a model with deep technical integration support). By comparing the effectiveness of these models on IIS adoption and use metrics across varied settings, the study directly tests which scalable training solution is most effective for overcoming the primary implementation challenge, providing an evidence-based blueprint for national rollout.

ideas.title: A Multi-Level, Tailored Implementation Strategy for IIS Training in Complex Healthcare Networks
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from agricultural extension science, which successfully disseminates new practices across diverse farming networks through a multi-level, tailored implementation strategy. The analogous problem of scaling a standardized intervention (new farming techniques) across varied local contexts is solved by combining a central core curriculum with localized adaptation and structured feedback. Adapted to the original idea, the research would evaluate an IIS training intervention that is not a one-size-fits-all program but a flexible framework. It would test the effectiveness of a model where a central training body provides the core evidence-based content to large chain pharmacies and integrated health systems, but then empowers local site champions or regional managers to tailor the delivery, timing, and EHR-specific integration steps. The study would measure effectiveness not only by learner outcomes but also by the efficiency of the feedback loop from local sites to the central body, assessing how this adaptive approach improves scalability and sustained implementation compared to a rigid, top-down training rollout.

ideas.title: Adapt academic detailing for IIS promotion
ideas.description: Use one-on-one educational outreach visits, common in prescribing, to promote IIS use to pharmacists.
ideas.sourceTitle: Increasing use of immunization information systems for routine vaccinations in independent community pharmacies: A randomized controlled trial

ideas.title: Inverting the Deficit Model: Reframing Pharmacy IIS Non-Participation as a Signal of Broader, Adaptive Operational Logic
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard public health informatics problem by synthesizing two perspectives. Instead of viewing pharmacies that refuse Immunization Information System (IIS) training or enrollment merely as problematic outliers resistant to change, we investigate whether their stance signals an alternative, adaptive operational logic. We test the hypothesis that what appears as non-compliance may be correlated with a broader, more systemic definition of pharmacy value and sustainability—akin to an 'altruistic' or community-systems view in engineering—that perceives the specific IIS intervention as misaligned with their holistic operational model. The goal is to identify characteristics of these pharmacies not as deficits to be corrected, but as indicators of a different institutional philosophy, thereby informing more effective engagement strategies that align with these broader logics.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Identify pharmacies resistant to training
ideas.description: Study characteristics of pharmacies that refuse IIS training or fail to enroll despite it.
ideas.sourceTitle: Increasing use of immunization information systems for routine vaccinations in independent community pharmacies: A randomized controlled trial
